> - **Module:** Module 1

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Introduction to Structural Dynamics

**Structural Dynamics** is the branch of structural engineering that deals with the analysis of structures subjected to **time-varying (dynamic) loads** and the resulting time-dependent displacements, velocities, accelerations, internal forces, and stresses.

When a load is applied *slowly* so that inertial effects (resistance to change in motion) are negligible, the problem is treated as **static**. When the load varies rapidly, the structure's mass cannot be ignored — the inertia of the mass produces forces that resist acceleration, and the response becomes **time-dependent**.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Definition (Verbatim Flavor):**
> *Structural Dynamics* is the study of the time-dependent behaviour of structures under dynamic loading, where the inertia and damping forces of the structure, in addition to the elastic and external forces, are taken into account.

### Conceptual Analogy

Imagine pushing a heavy bookshelf (mass = *m*). If you slowly nudge it, the shelf moves almost immediately to its new position — a **static** response. Now imagine the same bookshelf during an **earthquake**. The base suddenly shakes back and forth. The shelf, because of its inertia, lags behind the motion of the floor. The mass *resists* the rapid change of motion, and the shelf may even topple long after the initial shake. That resistance to change of motion is the **inertia force**, and the study of such effects is structural dynamics.

---

## 1.2 Static vs Dynamic Loading

| Property | Static Loading | Dynamic Loading |
|---|---|---|
| Time variation | Constant or slowly varying | Rapidly varying with time |
| Inertia forces | Negligible | **Must be considered** |
| Damping forces | Negligible | **Must be considered** |
| Equation form | $Ku = F$ | $M\ddot{u} + C\dot{u} + Ku = F(t)$ |
| Examples | Dead load, live load (quasi-static) | Earthquake, wind, blast, machine vibration |

---

## 1.3 Classification of Dynamic Loads

Dynamic loads are broadly classified into **two main categories**:

### A) Deterministic Loads (Predictable)
These can be expressed as a mathematical function of time. They are further subdivided:

#### 1. Periodic Loads
A load that repeats itself at regular intervals of time $T_p$.

**a) Simple Harmonic Load:**
$$F(t) = F_0 \sin(\omega t) \quad \text{or} \quad F_0 \cos(\omega t)$$
*Example:* Unbalanced rotating machinery, wind-induced vortex shedding.

**b) Complex (Non-Harmonic) Periodic Load:**
Any periodic load that is *not* pure sinusoidal but can be represented as a **Fourier series** of harmonics.
$$F(t) = a_0 + \sum_{n=1}^{\infty} \left[ a_n \cos(n\omega t) + b_n \sin(n\omega t) \right]$$
*Example:* Footstep loading on a pedestrian bridge, rotating machinery with multiple harmonics.

#### 2. Non-Periodic Loads
**a) Impulsive Load:**
A very large magnitude force applied over a *very short duration* (impulse duration $t_d \ll T_n$, the natural period of the structure). The shape of the load is unimportant; only the **impulse** $I = \int_0^{t_d} F(t)\, dt$ matters.
*Example:* Blast loading, hammer impact, projectile strike.

**b) Step / Suddenly Applied Load (Ramp):**
A load that is suddenly applied and held constant (or linearly increased). It is *not* impulsive because the load remains.
$$F(t) = \begin{cases} 0, & t < 0 \\ F_0, & t \geq 0 \end{cases}$$
*Example:* Sudden opening of a valve, dropped weight coming to rest.

### B) Non-Deterministic (Random) Loads
These loads cannot be described by a deterministic function of time; they are described by **statistical/probabilistic** measures (mean, variance, power spectral density).
*Example:* Earthquake ground motion, turbulent wind, ocean waves.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Focus:** Module 1 primarily deals with **deterministic dynamic loads** acting on **single-degree-of-freedom (SDOF)** systems. Random vibration and multi-DOF analysis come in later modules.

---

## 1.4 Essential Characteristics of a Dynamic Problem

For a problem to qualify as *dynamic*, **at least one** of the following characteristics must be present:

1. **The load is time-varying** — $F(t)$ rather than $F$ (constant).
2. **The load is suddenly applied** — even a constant load applied instantaneously produces dynamic effects (the structure oscillates before reaching static equilibrium).
3. **The mass of the structure is significant** — so that inertia forces $m\ddot{u}$ cannot be neglected.
4. **The structure is moving** — with significant velocity, leading to damping (energy dissipation) and inertia effects.

If a problem has **none** of these features, it is purely **static**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Highlight:** A clear understanding of *why* a problem is dynamic — not just *that* it is — is the foundation of every problem in this course. Examiners frequently ask for the justification.

---

## 1.5 Methods of Discretization (in Brief)

A real structure (a beam, a building, a bridge) has **infinite degrees of freedom** — its mass and elasticity are continuously distributed. To make the problem solvable, we **discretize** the system into a finite number of degrees of freedom. The principal methods are:

| Method | Concept | Key Use |
|---|---|---|
| **Lumped-Mass (Concentrated Mass)** | Mass of distributed elements is *concentrated* at nodal points; elements themselves are massless. | Buildings (slabs lumped at floor levels), simple beams. |
| **Generalized (Rayleigh-Ritz)** | Assume a shape function $\psi(x)$; reduce to few coordinates. | Beams, plates where continuous elasticity is preserved. |
| **Finite Element Method (FEM)** | Domain is divided into elements; mass and stiffness matrices are computed for each element and assembled. | Complex 2D/3D structures. |
| **Consistent Mass Method** | Mass distribution is represented by a *consistent* mass matrix derived from shape functions. | More accurate than lumped mass, used in FEM. |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> For Module 1 (SDOF), the **lumped-mass idealization** is the primary method. A typical example: a single-story building frame represented as a mass $m$ on a massless column of stiffness $k$.

---

## 1.6 Single Degree of Freedom (SDOF) System

A **Single Degree of Freedom (SDOF)** system is one whose configuration at any instant is completely defined by a **single coordinate** $u(t)$. Equivalently, the mass can move in only **one direction**, and the displacement is the same throughout the system.

**Classic Examples:**
- A mass $m$ attached to a spring of stiffness $k$ and a dashpot of damping $c$ on a smooth surface.
- A single-story building modeled as a mass on a flexible column.
- A simple pendulum for small oscillations.

### Mathematical Definition
$$u(t) \in \mathbb{R}^1 \quad \text{— one scalar coordinate fully describes the position.}$$

---

## 1.7 Basic Components of a Dynamic (SDOF) System

Every dynamic system is built from **three essential physical components**:

| Component | Symbol | Physical Meaning | Unit (SI) |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Mass (Inertia)** | $m$ | Property that resists change in motion. Stores kinetic energy. | kg |
| **Stiffness (Elasticity)** | $k$ | Property that resists deformation. Stores potential energy. | N/m |
| **Damping (Energy Dissipation)** | $c$ | Property that dissipates energy (e.g., viscous dashpot). | N·s/m |
| **External Force (Excitation)** | $F(t)$ | The applied time-varying load. | N |

### Visual Intuition
- **Mass $m$** → the "inertia" of a heavy block.
- **Spring $k$** → the "elastic restoring" element trying to pull the block back to equilibrium.
- **Dashpot $c$** → a shock-absorber that "resists velocity" and bleeds energy.
- **Force $F(t)$** → the "pusher" (earthquake, wind, etc.).

---

## 1.8 Formulation of the Equation of Motion (EOM) — Overview

The **Equation of Motion** is a second-order ordinary differential equation (ODE) that describes how the coordinate $u(t)$ of a dynamic system evolves in time. For an SDOF system:

$$m\ddot{u}(t) + c\dot{u}(t) + k u(t) = F(t)$$

This single equation, with appropriate initial conditions $u(0)$ and $\dot{u}(0)$, fully characterizes the dynamic response of the SDOF system.

The two principal ways to derive this equation are:
1. **Newton's Second Law of Motion** ($F = ma$).
2. **D'Alembert's Principle** (equilibrium of forces including a fictitious *inertia force*).

Both are explored in detail in the next section.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Newton's Second Law of Motion (Direct Approach)

**Statement:** The time rate of change of linear momentum of a body equals the net external force acting on it.
$$F_{net} = \frac{d}{dt}(m v) = m a = m \ddot{u}$$
(For a constant mass system — which is the assumption throughout this module.)

### Application to SDOF System
**Step 1 — Free Body Diagram (FBD):** Draw the mass $m$ as a free body. Identify all forces acting *along the direction of motion* $u(t)$:
- **Spring force:** Always opposing displacement → $F_s = -k u(t)$
- **Damping force:** Always opposing velocity → $F_d = -c \dot{u}(t)$
- **Applied (external) force:** As given → $F(t)$

**Step 2 — Apply Newton's Second Law:**
$$F_{net} = m \ddot{u}(t)$$
$$F(t) - c \dot{u}(t) - k u(t) = m \ddot{u}(t)$$

**Step 3 — Rearrange to Standard Form:**
$$m \ddot{u}(t) + c \dot{u}(t) + k u(t) = F(t)$$

This is the **governing EOM of a forced, damped SDOF system** — the central equation of the course.

### Unforced (Free Vibration) Special Case
If $F(t) = 0$:
$$m \ddot{u} + c \dot{u} + k u = 0$$

If additionally $c = 0$ (undamped):
$$m \ddot{u} + k u = 0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \ddot{u} + \omega_n^2 u = 0$$
where $\omega_n = \sqrt{k/m}$ is the **natural (circular) frequency** in rad/s.

---

## 2.2 D'Alembert's Principle (Equilibrium / Inertia Force Approach)

**Statement (Jean le Rond d'Alembert, 1743):** A dynamic system can be treated as being in **instantaneous static equilibrium** under the action of the real external forces *plus* a fictitious force called the **inertia force** (or *reversed effective force*), defined as $F_I = -m\ddot{u}$.

In other words, we *reverse* Newton's 2nd law: instead of $F_{net} = m a$, we write
$$\sum F_{real} + F_I = 0 \quad \text{where} \quad F_I = -m\ddot{u}$$

### Physical Interpretation
The inertia force is *not* a real force — it is a mathematical artifice. It represents the force the mass *exerts backward* as it is accelerated by the real forces. In static equilibrium, $\ddot{u} = 0$, so $F_I = 0$ and we recover statics.

### Application to SDOF System
Draw the FBD as before, but now include the **inertia force** $F_I = -m\ddot{u}(t)$ acting on the mass in the direction *opposite* to its acceleration.

For equilibrium:
$$F(t) - c \dot{u} - k u - m \ddot{u} = 0$$
$$\Rightarrow m \ddot{u}(t) + c \dot{u}(t) + k u(t) = F(t)$$

> [!NOTE]
> The resulting equation is **identical** to that obtained via Newton's 2nd law. The two methods are mathematically equivalent. D'Alembert's approach is preferred in formal mechanics of materials and energy methods because it extends elegantly to multi-DOF systems (e.g., using virtual work with inertia forces).

---

## 2.3 Comparison: Newton's 2nd Law vs D'Alembert's Principle

| Aspect | Newton's 2nd Law | D'Alembert's Principle |
|---|---|---|
| Form | $F_{net} = m a$ | $F_{net} + F_I = 0$ |
| Inertia force | Implicit (on RHS) | Explicit (added as fictitious force) |
| Mental model | Dynamic (cause produces motion) | Quasi-static (instantaneous equilibrium) |
| Best for | Simple SDOF, direct application | Multi-DOF, energy methods, FEM |

---

## 2.4 Special Cases of the SDOF EOM

| Case | Equation | Solution Type |
|---|---|---|
| Undamped Free | $\ddot{u} + \omega_n^2 u = 0$ | Pure sinusoid: $u(t) = A\cos\omega_n t + B\sin\omega_n t$ |
| Damped Free | $\ddot{u} + 2\zeta\omega_n \dot{u} + \omega_n^2 u = 0$ | Exponential decay (under/critically/over-damped) |
| Forced (harmonic) | $\ddot{u} + 2\zeta\omega_n \dot{u} + \omega_n^2 u = (F_0/m)\sin\omega t$ | Steady-state + transient |
| Forced (general) | $\ddot{u} + 2\zeta\omega_n \dot{u} + \omega_n^2 u = F(t)/m$ | Duhamel integral |

Definitions used:
- $\omega_n = \sqrt{k/m}$ — natural circular frequency (rad/s)
- $\zeta = c / (2\sqrt{km}) = c / c_{cr}$ — damping ratio
- $c_{cr} = 2\sqrt{km} = 2m\omega_n$ — critical damping coefficient

---

## 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Formula / Concept | Expression | Unit / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | General SDOF EOM | $m\ddot{u} + c\dot{u} + ku = F(t)$ | kg, N·s/m, N/m, N |
| 2 | Newton's 2nd Law | $F_{net} = m\ddot{u}$ | Scalar; constant mass |
| 3 | D'Alembert's Inertia Force | $F_I = -m\ddot{u}$ | Fictitious; opposes acceleration |
| 4 | Spring Force | $F_s = -ku$ | Restoring (Hookean) |
| 5 | Viscous Damping Force | $F_d = -c\dot{u}$ | Opposes velocity |
| 6 | Natural Frequency | $\omega_n = \sqrt{k/m}$ | rad/s |
| 7 | Natural Period | $T_n = 2\pi\sqrt{m/k}$ | seconds |
| 8 | Natural Frequency (Hz) | $f_n = \omega_n / (2\pi)$ | Hz |
| 9 | Critical Damping | $c_{cr} = 2\sqrt{km} = 2m\omega_n$ | N·s/m |
| 10 | Damping Ratio | $\zeta = c / c_{cr}$ | Dimensionless |
| 11 | Stiffness (axial bar) | $k = EA/L$ | N/m |
| 12 | Stiffness (cantilever tip) | $k = 3EI/L^3$ | N/m |
| 13 | Stiffness (simply supported, mid-load) | $k = 48EI/L^3$ | N/m |
| 14 | Static Displacement | $u_{st} = F_0 / k$ | m (under static load $F_0$) |
| 15 | Dynamic Load Factor (DLF) | $\text{DLF} = u_{max}^{dyn} / u_{st}$ | Dimensionless (for sudden/impulsive loads) |
| 16 | DLF (sudden step load) | $\text{DLF} = 2$ | (Undamped SDOF) |
| 17 | DLF (rectangular impulse, $t_d/T_n = \tau$) | $\text{DLF} = 2\sin(\pi\tau)$ | Valid for $0 \le \tau \le 1/2$ |

> [!WARNING]
> Always include units in numerical answers. KTU examiners deduct marks for missing units in the final step.

---

## 2.6 Real-World Utility in Engineering

The EOM of an SDOF system underlies the design of:
- **Buildings** subjected to earthquakes (the mass of floors lumped on columns).
- **Chimneys, towers, water tanks** under wind gusts.
- **Vehicle suspension systems** (mass-spring-damper).
- **Machine tool foundations** to isolate vibration.
- **Pedestrian bridges** subjected to footfall loading.
- **Blast-resistant structures** under impulsive loading.

The concepts of *natural period*, *damping ratio*, and *dynamic load factor* are the first tools an engineer uses to decide whether a structure will respond safely or dangerously to a given dynamic input.

# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Equation of Motion of an Undamped SDOF System

**System:** A rigid block of mass $m$ attached to a massless spring of stiffness $k$, free to slide on a frictionless surface, with the other end of the spring fixed to a wall. Displace the mass by $u(t)$ from equilibrium and release.

**Step 1 — Identify the Coordinate:**
Let $u(t)$ be the displacement of the mass measured from the *static equilibrium* position (where the spring force is zero). This choice automatically cancels the gravity term in vertical problems.

**Step 2 — Free Body Diagram (FBD):**
On the mass, only the **spring force** acts along the direction of motion.
- The spring is stretched (or compressed) by $u(t)$.
- The spring pulls the mass back toward equilibrium.

**Step 3 — Apply Newton's Second Law:**
$$\sum F_x = m \ddot{u}$$
$$-k\, u(t) = m \ddot{u}(t)$$

**Step 4 — Rearrange to Standard Form:**
$$m \ddot{u}(t) + k u(t) = 0$$

**Step 5 — Divide by $m$:**
$$\ddot{u}(t) + \frac{k}{m} u(t) = 0$$

**Step 6 — Define Natural Frequency:**
$$\omega_n^2 \equiv \frac{k}{m} \quad \Rightarrow \quad \omega_n = \sqrt{\frac{k}{m}}$$

**Step 7 — Final Equation:**
$$\ddot{u}(t) + \omega_n^2\, u(t) = 0$$

This is the **simple harmonic oscillator equation** whose general solution is
$$u(t) = A \cos \omega_n t + B \sin \omega_n t = C \sin(\omega_n t + \phi)$$
where $A, B$ (or $C, \phi$) are determined by initial conditions.

**Derivation of $\omega_n$ for vertical SDOF (with gravity):**
Let $u$ be measured from the *unstretched* spring position. Then the spring stretch is $(u + \delta_{st})$ where $\delta_{st} = mg/k$.
$$m\ddot{u} = -k(u + \delta_{st}) + mg = -k u - k \cdot \frac{mg}{k} + mg = -ku$$
Same equation as before — gravity cancels. Hence the natural frequency is independent of gravity, and choosing equilibrium as the reference simplifies the EOM.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Equation of Motion of a Damped Forced SDOF System

**System:** Mass $m$, spring $k$, **viscous dashpot** $c$ (resists velocity), external force $F(t)$ applied to the mass.

**Step 1 — Coordinate:** Let $u(t)$ be the displacement of the mass from equilibrium.

**Step 2 — FBD — Forces on the Mass in the Direction of $u$:**
- Spring force: $-k u(t)$ (opposes displacement)
- Damping force: $-c \dot{u}(t)$ (opposes velocity)
- Applied force: $+F(t)$

**Step 3 — Newton's Second Law:**
$$m \ddot{u}(t) = -k u(t) - c \dot{u}(t) + F(t)$$

**Step 4 — Rearrange to Standard Form:**
$$m \ddot{u}(t) + c \dot{u}(t) + k u(t) = F(t)$$

**Step 5 — Divide by $m$ and Introduce Standard Parameters:**
$$\ddot{u} + \frac{c}{m}\dot{u} + \frac{k}{m}u = \frac{F(t)}{m}$$
$$\ddot{u} + 2\zeta\omega_n \dot{u} + \omega_n^2 u = \frac{F(t)}{m}$$
where $2\zeta\omega_n = c/m$ and $\omega_n^2 = k/m$, so $\zeta = c/(2m\omega_n) = c/(2\sqrt{km})$.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3: D'Alembert's Principle (Detailed)

**Statement:** A mass in accelerated motion can be treated as being in instantaneous *static* equilibrium if we add a fictitious **inertia force** $F_I = -m\ddot{u}$ to the system of real forces.

**Step 1 — Begin with Newton's Second Law:**
$$F_{net} = m \ddot{u}$$

**Step 2 — Move All Terms to One Side:**
$$F_{net} - m \ddot{u} = 0$$

**Step 3 — Define Inertia Force:**
$$F_I \equiv -m \ddot{u}$$

**Step 4 — Substitute:**
$$F_{net} + F_I = 0$$

This is the **D'Alembert equation** — a *static equilibrium* statement for a *dynamic* problem. The mass is "held in equilibrium" by the real forces *plus* the fictitious inertia force.

**Step 5 — Apply to SDOF System:**
Real forces: $F(t)$ (applied), $-k u$ (spring), $-c \dot{u}$ (damping).
$$F(t) - k u - c \dot{u} - m \ddot{u} = 0$$
$$m \ddot{u} + c \dot{u} + k u = F(t) \quad \text{(identical to Newton's method)}$$

**Why Both Methods Give the Same Result:**
Newton's 2nd law treats the inertia as a *property* on the right side ($ma$). D'Alembert treats the inertia as a *force* on the left side. They are algebraically equivalent — purely a matter of bookkeeping.

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4: Natural Frequency for Common Configurations

### (a) Mass on Spring (Horizontal)
$$\omega_n = \sqrt{\frac{k}{m}}$$

### (b) Vertical Mass on Spring (gravity cancels)
$$\omega_n = \sqrt{\frac{k}{m}}$$

### (c) Simple Pendulum (small oscillations)
For a point mass $m$ on a string of length $L$, restoring torque $= -mg\sin\theta \approx -mg\theta$, and rotational inertia $= mL^2$. Equation:
$$mL^2 \ddot{\theta} + mg L \theta = 0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \omega_n = \sqrt{\frac{g}{L}}$$

### (d) SDOF Cantilever Beam with Tip Mass
Tip stiffness of a cantilever of length $L$, flexural rigidity $EI$, loaded at the tip:
$$k = \frac{3EI}{L^3}$$
$$\omega_n = \sqrt{\frac{3EI}{m L^3}}$$

### (e) SDOF Simply Supported Beam with Central Mass
For a simply supported beam of length $L$ with a mass $m$ at the center, the deflection at the center under a central load is $\delta = PL^3 / (48EI)$, so:
$$k = \frac{48EI}{L^3}$$
$$\omega_n = \sqrt{\frac{48EI}{m L^3}}$$

### (f) Multi-Story Building (Lumped Mass Approximation)
A single-story frame with floor mass $m$ and column stiffness $k_{col}$ (each column contributes $12EI/h^3$; two columns → $k = 24EI/h^3$ for a 2-column bent):
$$\omega_n = \sqrt{\frac{k_{total}}{m}} = \sqrt{\frac{24 EI}{m h^3}} \quad \text{(2-column frame, height h)}$$

---

## 3.5 Derivation 5: Damping Ratio and Critical Damping

**Starting Point (Free Damped Equation):**
$$m \ddot{u} + c \dot{u} + k u = 0$$

**Step 1 — Assume a Solution of the Form:**
$$u(t) = e^{\lambda t}$$

**Step 2 — Substitute:**
$$m \lambda^2 e^{\lambda t} + c \lambda e^{\lambda t} + k e^{\lambda t} = 0$$
$$(m \lambda^2 + c \lambda + k) e^{\lambda t} = 0$$

**Step 3 — Characteristic Equation:**
$$m \lambda^2 + c \lambda + k = 0$$

**Step 4 — Solve for Roots:**
$$\lambda = \frac{-c \pm \sqrt{c^2 - 4mk}}{2m}$$

**Step 5 — Critical Damping ($c_{cr}$):**
Critical damping is the value of $c$ for which the discriminant is zero — i.e., the boundary between oscillatory and non-oscillatory response.
$$c_{cr}^2 - 4mk = 0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad c_{cr} = 2\sqrt{mk}$$

**Step 6 — Damping Ratio:**
$$\zeta \equiv \frac{c}{c_{cr}} = \frac{c}{2\sqrt{mk}} = \frac{c}{2 m \omega_n}$$

**Three Cases:**
- $\zeta < 1$ → **Underdamped** (oscillatory, decaying)
- $\zeta = 1$ → **Critically damped** (fastest non-oscillatory return)
- $\zeta > 1$ → **Overdamped** (slow non-oscillatory return)

---

## 3.6 Analytical Summary Matrix

| System | EOM | Solution Form | Key Parameter |
|---|---|---|---|
| Undamped Free | $\ddot{u} + \omega_n^2 u = 0$ | $u = A\cos\omega_n t + B\sin\omega_n t$ | $\omega_n = \sqrt{k/m}$ |
| Damped Free | $\ddot{u} + 2\zeta\omega_n \dot{u} + \omega_n^2 u = 0$ | $u = e^{-\zeta\omega_n t}(A\cos\omega_d t + B\sin\omega_d t)$ | $\omega_d = \omega_n\sqrt{1-\zeta^2}$ |
| Forced, undamped | $m\ddot{u} + ku = F_0\sin\omega t$ | $u = u_h + (F_0/k)\cdot \frac{1}{1-r^2}\sin\omega t$ | $r = \omega/\omega_n$ |
| Forced, damped | $m\ddot{u} + c\dot{u} + ku = F_0\sin\omega t$ | Steady-state amplitude: $u_0 = \frac{F_0/k}{\sqrt{(1-r^2)^2 + (2\zeta r)^2}}$ | Magnification factor |

# SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 SDOF System — Idealized Block-Level Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    wallA[Fixed Wall]:::anchor -->|anchored| springK[Spring Stiffness k]
    springK -->|restoring force Fs = -k u| massNode[Mass m]
    massNode -->|inertia F I = -m ddot u| massNode
    dashC[Dashpot Damping c]:::damper -->|damping force Fd = -c dot u| massNode
    forceF[External Force F of t]:::load -->|drives| massNode
    massNode -->|output displacement u of t| outputNode[Response u t, v t, a t]:::output

    classDef anchor fill:#1e3a5f,stroke:#0b1e30,color:#ffffff,stroke-width:2px
    classDef damper fill:#5a3825,stroke:#2a1a10,color:#ffffff,stroke-width:2px
    classDef load fill:#7a1f2b,stroke:#400a14,color:#ffffff,stroke-width:2px
    classDef output fill:#1f5f3a,stroke:#0a2a18,color:#ffffff,stroke-width:2px
```

**How to read this diagram:**
- The **wall** anchors the system (immovable reference).
- The **spring** provides the elastic restoring force.
- The **dashpot** dissipates energy through viscous action.
- The **external force** drives the system.
- The **mass** is the central element; it carries inertia, connects to spring and dashpot, and produces the response.

---

## 4.2 Classification Tree of Dynamic Loads

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    rootNode[Dynamic Loads]:::root --> detA[Deterministic]
    rootNode --> nondetA[Non Deterministic Random]:::rand

    detA --> perA[Periodic]:::per
    detA --> nonperA[Non Periodic]:::nonper

    perA --> simpleA[Simple Harmonic F0 sin omega t]:::per
    perA --> complexA[Complex Periodic Fourier Series]:::per

    nonperA --> impA[Impulsive Short Duration Large Magnitude]:::nonper
    nonperA --> stepA[Step Suddenly Applied Constant]:::nonper

    nondetA --> earthA[Earthquake Ground Motion]:::rand
    nondetA --> windA[Turbulent Wind]:::rand
    nondetA --> waveA[Ocean Wave Loading]:::rand

    classDef root fill:#1e3a5f,stroke:#0b1e30,color:#ffffff,stroke-width:2px
    classDef per fill:#2d4a73,stroke:#15233d,color:#ffffff,stroke-width:2px
    classDef nonper fill:#734a2d,stroke:#3d2315,color:#ffffff,stroke-width:2px
    classDef rand fill:#5f1f3a,stroke:#2a0a18,color:#ffffff,stroke-width:2px
```

---

## 4.3 Components of a Dynamic Problem — Functional Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    inputNode[Dynamic Load F of t]:::input
    massSys[Mass m]:::mass
    stiffSys[Stiffness k]:::stiff
    dampSys[Damping c]:::damp

    inputNode -->|drives| eqnNode[Equation of Motion]:::eqn
    massSys -->|inertia force| eqnNode
    stiffSys -->|restoring force| eqnNode
    dampSys -->|damping force| eqnNode

    eqnNode -->|solve| respNode[Response u t]:::resp
    respNode --> dispN[Displacement u t]:::out
    respNode --> velN[Velocity dot u t]:::out
    respNode --> accN[Acceleration ddot u t]:::out

    classDef input fill:#7a1f2b,stroke:#400a14,color:#ffffff,stroke-width:2px
    classDef mass fill:#1e3a5f,stroke:#0b1e30,color:#ffffff,stroke-width:2px
    classDef stiff fill:#2d5f1f,stroke:#13300a,color:#ffffff,stroke-width:2px
    classDef damp fill:#5a3825,stroke:#2a1a10,color:#ffffff,stroke-width:2px
    classDef eqn fill:#5f1f5f,stroke:#2a0a2a,color:#ffffff,stroke-width:2px
    classDef resp fill:#1f5f3a,stroke:#0a2a18,color:#ffffff,stroke-width:2px
    classDef out fill:#333333,stroke:#000000,color:#ffffff,stroke-width:2px
```

---

## 4.4 Newton's 2nd Law vs D'Alembert's — Side-by-Side Logic

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    newtonPath[Newton 2nd Law]:::n --> n1[F net equals m ddot u]:::n
    n1 --> n2[Rearrange to standard form]:::n
    n2 --> n3[EOM: m ddot u plus c dot u plus k u equals F of t]:::eom

    dalembertPath[D Alembert Principle]:::d --> d1[Define inertia force F I equals minus m ddot u]:::d
    d1 --> d2[Static equilibrium: F real plus F I equals 0]:::d
    d2 --> d3[EOM: m ddot u plus c dot u plus k u equals F of t]:::eom

    classDef n fill:#1e3a5f,stroke:#0b1e30,color:#ffffff,stroke-width:2px
    classDef d fill:#5a3825,stroke:#2a1a10,color:#ffffff,stroke-width:2px
    classDef eom fill:#1f5f3a,stroke:#0a2a18,color:#ffffff,stroke-width:3px
```

**Key insight:** Both methods *converge* on the **same** equation of motion. The difference is purely in *bookkeeping* — D'Alembert treats inertia as a *force* (left side); Newton treats inertia as a *property* (right side).

---

## 4.5 Discretization Methods — Decision Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    startN[Continuous Structure]:::start --> q1{Number of DOF needed for accuracy?}
    q1 -->|Few| lumpedA[Lumped Mass Method]:::lump
    q1 -->|Many or distributed| rayA[Generalized Coordinates Rayleigh Ritz]:::ray
    q1 -->|Complex geometry| femA[Finite Element Method]:::fem

    lumpedA --> lk1[Concentrate mass at nodes; elements massless]:::lump
    rayA --> rk1[Assume shape function psi of x; reduce to few coordinates]:::ray
    femA --> fk1[Discretize into elements; assemble mass and stiffness matrices]:::fem

    classDef start fill:#1e3a5f,stroke:#0b1e30,color:#ffffff,stroke-width:2px
    classDef lump fill:#2d5f1f,stroke:#13300a,color:#ffffff,stroke-width:2px
    classDef ray fill:#5f4a1f,stroke:#2a200a,color:#ffffff,stroke-width:2px
    classDef fem fill:#5f1f3a,stroke:#2a0a18,color:#ffffff,stroke-width:2px
```

# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **Q1.** [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Module 1]
> *Differentiate between static and dynamic loading with examples. Under what condition does a suddenly applied constant load produce a dynamic (transient) effect?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Static Loading | Dynamic Loading |
|---|---|
| Load is constant or varies very slowly with time. | Load varies rapidly with time. |
| Inertia forces are negligible. | Inertia forces are significant. |
| Equation: $Ku = F$ | Equation: $m\ddot{u} + c\dot{u} + ku = F(t)$ |
| Example: Dead load on a roof slab | Example: Earthquake ground motion on a building |

A **suddenly applied constant load** (e.g., a weight dropped suddenly onto a beam) produces dynamic effects because the structure *transiently oscillates* before reaching static equilibrium. The mass cannot respond instantaneously; it overshoots the static deflection. *(1 mark for condition, 2 marks for table.)*

---

> **Q2.** [KTU University Exam — June 2024, Module 1]
> *State D'Alembert's principle. How is the inertia force defined, and how does it differ from a real force?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **D'Alembert's Principle:** A dynamic system can be treated as being in instantaneous static equilibrium under the action of real external forces plus a fictitious **inertia force** $F_I = -m\ddot{u}$. *(1 mark)*
- **Inertia force definition:** $F_I = -m\ddot{u}$, directed opposite to the acceleration of the mass. It is *not* a real force; it is a mathematical construct that allows dynamic problems to be solved using static equilibrium methods. *(1 mark)*
- **Difference from real force:** Real forces are measurable (spring, damping, applied). Inertia force is *fictitious* — it represents the resistance of mass to acceleration. It vanishes when $\ddot{u} = 0$ (i.e., in statics). *(1 mark)*

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

> **Q3.** [KTU University Exam — Model Question, Module 1, 14 Marks]
> **(a)** A machine of mass $m = 500$ kg is mounted on a foundation that behaves as a spring of stiffness $k = 2 \times 10^6$ N/m. The machine has a dashpot providing viscous damping with $c = 4000$ N·s/m. **(i)** Formulate the equation of motion of the system when subjected to a time-varying force $F(t) = 1000 \sin(50t)$ N. **(ii)** Calculate the natural frequency $\omega_n$, the damping ratio $\zeta$, and the damped natural frequency $\omega_d$. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** Using **D'Alembert's principle**, derive the equation of motion of a damped SDOF system subjected to a harmonic force $F(t) = F_0 \sin \omega t$. Show that the resulting equation is identical to the one obtained from Newton's second law. **(7 marks)**

### **MODEL SOLUTION**

#### Part (a) — Numerical Formulation (7 marks)

**Given:** $m = 500$ kg, $k = 2 \times 10^6$ N/m, $c = 4000$ N·s/m, $F(t) = 1000 \sin(50t)$ N.

**Step 1 — Formulate the EOM:**
Using Newton's 2nd law on the mass (FBD with spring, dashpot, applied force):
$$m\ddot{u} + c\dot{u} + k u = F(t)$$
$$\boxed{500\,\ddot{u} + 4000\,\dot{u} + (2 \times 10^6)\, u = 1000 \sin(50t)}$$
*(2 marks for substitution)*

**Step 2 — Natural Frequency:**
$$\omega_n = \sqrt{\frac{k}{m}} = \sqrt{\frac{2 \times 10^6}{500}} = \sqrt{4000} = 63.25 \text{ rad/s}$$
*(1 mark)*

**Step 3 — Damping Ratio:**
First, critical damping:
$$c_{cr} = 2\sqrt{km} = 2\sqrt{2 \times 10^6 \times 500} = 2 \times 31622.8 = 63245.5 \text{ N·s/m}$$
$$\zeta = \frac{c}{c_{cr}} = \frac{4000}{63245.5} = 0.0632 \; (\approx 6.32\%)$$
*(2 marks)*

**Step 4 — Damped Natural Frequency:**
$$\omega_d = \omega_n \sqrt{1 - \zeta^2} = 63.25 \times \sqrt{1 - 0.0040} = 63.25 \times 0.9980 = 63.12 \text{ rad/s}$$
*(1 mark)*

**Alternative — using $2\zeta\omega_n = c/m$ check:**
$$2\zeta\omega_n = \frac{c}{m} = \frac{4000}{500} = 8 \text{ s}^{-1} \quad \Rightarrow \quad \zeta = \frac{8}{2 \times 63.25} = 0.0632 \;\checkmark$$

---

#### Part (b) — Derivation via D'Alembert's Principle (7 marks)

**Step 1 — State D'Alembert's Principle:** *(1 mark)*
> *A system in accelerated motion can be treated as in instantaneous static equilibrium if a fictitious inertia force $F_I = -m\ddot{u}$ is added to the real forces.*

**Step 2 — Identify Real Forces on the Mass:** *(1 mark)*
- Spring force: $F_s = -k u(t)$ (opposes displacement)
- Damping force: $F_d = -c \dot{u}(t)$ (opposes velocity)
- Applied force: $F(t) = F_0 \sin \omega t$

**Step 3 — Define Inertia Force:** *(1 mark)*
$$F_I = -m\ddot{u}(t)$$

**Step 4 — Apply Static Equilibrium ($\sum F = 0$):** *(2 marks)*
$$F(t) + F_s + F_d + F_I = 0$$
$$F_0 \sin\omega t - k u - c \dot{u} - m \ddot{u} = 0$$

**Step 5 — Rearrange to Standard Form:** *(1 mark)*
$$m \ddot{u}(t) + c \dot{u}(t) + k u(t) = F_0 \sin \omega t$$

**Step 6 — Equivalence to Newton's Result:** *(1 mark)*
Compare with Newton's 2nd law derivation:
$$m\ddot{u} = -ku - c\dot{u} + F_0 \sin\omega t$$
Both give the **identical EOM**. The two methods differ only in *bookkeeping*: D'Alembert moves inertia to the LHS as a fictitious force, while Newton keeps it on the RHS as $m\ddot{u}$.

---

### **Alternative Question (Internal Choice B) — 14 Marks**

> **Q3 (Alt).** [KTU University Exam — Model Question, Module 1, 14 Marks]
> **(a)** A simply supported steel beam of length $L = 4$ m, flexural rigidity $EI = 8 \times 10^6$ N·m² carries a heavy mass $m = 2000$ kg at its centre. The beam is modelled as a massless spring. **(i)** Determine the stiffness of the spring equivalent. **(ii)** Formulate the equation of motion using **Newton's second law** if a suddenly applied force $F(t) = 5000$ N acts on the mass. **(iii)** Compute the natural period $T_n$. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** A single-degree-of-freedom system has mass $m = 100$ kg, stiffness $k = 40000$ N/m, and damping coefficient $c = 200$ N·s/m. The system is subjected to a force $F(t) = 1000 \cos(20t)$ N. **(i)** Write the equation of motion in standard form. **(ii)** Determine whether the system is underdamped, critically damped, or overdamped. **(iii)** Calculate the damped natural frequency. **(7 marks)**

#### Part (a) Model Solution (7 marks)

**(i) Stiffness:** For a simply supported beam with central load:
$$k = \frac{48 EI}{L^3} = \frac{48 \times 8 \times 10^6}{4^3} = \frac{384 \times 10^6}{64} = 6 \times 10^6 \text{ N/m}$$
*(2 marks)*

**(ii) EOM via Newton's 2nd Law:** Let $u(t)$ be the downward displacement of the mass from equilibrium.
Forces on mass: spring force $-ku$, applied force $F(t) = 5000$ N.
$$m\ddot{u} = -ku + F(t)$$
$$2000\,\ddot{u} + 6 \times 10^6\, u = 5000$$
*(2 marks)*

**(iii) Natural Period:**
$$\omega_n = \sqrt{\frac{k}{m}} = \sqrt{\frac{6 \times 10^6}{2000}} = \sqrt{3000} = 54.77 \text{ rad/s}$$
$$T_n = \frac{2\pi}{\omega_n} = \frac{2\pi}{54.77} = 0.1147 \text{ s}$$
*(2 marks, 1 for $\omega_n$ and 1 for $T_n$)*

#### Part (b) Model Solution (7 marks)

**(i) Standard EOM:**
$$100\,\ddot{u} + 200\,\dot{u} + 40000\, u = 1000 \cos(20t)$$
*(1 mark)*

**(ii) Damping Check:**
$$\omega_n = \sqrt{\frac{k}{m}} = \sqrt{\frac{40000}{100}} = 20 \text{ rad/s}$$
$$c_{cr} = 2\sqrt{km} = 2\sqrt{40000 \times 100} = 2 \times 2000 = 4000 \text{ N·s/m}$$
$$\zeta = \frac{c}{c_{cr}} = \frac{200}{4000} = 0.05$$
Since $\zeta = 0.05 < 1$, the system is **underdamped**. *(3 marks: 1 for $\omega_n$, 1 for $c_{cr}$ and $\zeta$, 1 for conclusion)*

**(iii) Damped Natural Frequency:**
$$\omega_d = \omega_n \sqrt{1 - \zeta^2} = 20 \times \sqrt{1 - 0.0025} = 20 \times 0.9987 = 19.975 \text{ rad/s}$$
*(2 marks)*

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Common Mark-Deduction Points:**
> 1. **Forgetting units** in the final answer. Always state "rad/s" for $\omega_n$ and "seconds" for $T_n$.
> 2. **Measuring $u(t)$ from the unstretched position** in vertical problems — this *does not* change the EOM (gravity cancels), but students often add an extra $mg$ term incorrectly. Reference must be **static equilibrium**.
> 3. **Sign errors on the spring and damping forces.** Both oppose *motion*, not *applied force*. They appear with a **minus** sign in the EOM.
> 4. **Confusing natural frequency** ($\omega_n$, rad/s) with **cyclic frequency** ($f_n$, Hz). They are related by $\omega_n = 2\pi f_n$.
> 5. **D'Alembert's inertia force** must have a **minus sign**: $F_I = -m\ddot{u}$. Writing $F_I = +m\ddot{u}$ loses 1 mark and leads to a sign-flipped EOM.
> 6. **For critical damping**, the formula is $c_{cr} = 2\sqrt{mk} = 2m\omega_n$, *not* $2k\omega_n$.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Module 1 (SDOF Foundations)**

### Core Definitions
- ☐ **Static vs Dynamic:** Dynamic = time-varying load OR inertia significant OR suddenly applied load.
- ☐ **Dynamic Load Types:** Periodic (harmonic, complex) | Non-periodic (impulsive, step) | Random.
- ☐ **SDOF:** One coordinate $u(t)$ fully describes the configuration.
- ☐ **Lumped mass:** Discretize a continuous system by concentrating mass at discrete points.

### The Four Components of an SDOF System
- ☐ **Mass** $m$ (kg) — inertia, stores kinetic energy.
- ☐ **Stiffness** $k$ (N/m) — elasticity, stores potential energy.
- ☐ **Damping** $c$ (N·s/m) — viscous energy dissipation.
- ☐ **Excitation** $F(t)$ (N) — applied time-varying load.

### The Master Equation (Memorize!)
- ☐ $$\boxed{m\ddot{u} + c\dot{u} + k u = F(t)}$$

### Key Derived Quantities
- ☐ $\omega_n = \sqrt{k/m}$ (rad/s)
- ☐ $T_n = 2\pi/\omega_n$ (s)
- ☐ $f_n = \omega_n/(2\pi)$ (Hz)
- ☐ $c_{cr} = 2\sqrt{km} = 2m\omega_n$
- ☐ $\zeta = c/c_{cr} = c/(2m\omega_n)$
- ☐ $\omega_d = \omega_n\sqrt{1-\zeta^2}$ (underdamped case)

### The Two Derivation Methods
- ☐ **Newton's 2nd Law:** $F_{net} = m\ddot{u}$ → Rearrange to standard form.
- ☐ **D'Alembert's Principle:** $F_{real} + F_I = 0$ where $F_I = -m\ddot{u}$ (fictitious inertia force).
- ☐ **Both give the SAME EOM** — they are mathematically equivalent.

### Common Stiffness Formulas (KTU favourites)
- ☐ Cantilever tip load: $k = 3EI/L^3$
- ☐ Simply supported, central load: $k = 48EI/L^3$
- ☐ Axial bar: $k = EA/L$
- ☐ Two-column building bent: $k = 24EI/h^3$

### Damping Behaviour
- ☐ $\zeta < 1$ → **Underdamped** (oscillatory decay)
- ☐ $\zeta = 1$ → **Critically damped** (fastest non-oscillatory return)
- ☐ $\zeta > 1$ → **Overdamped** (slow non-oscillatory return)

### Final-Exam Golden Rules
- ☐ Always pick $u(t)$ **from static equilibrium** (gravity cancels automatically).
- ☐ Always sign spring force as $-ku$ and damping force as $-c\dot{u}$ in the EOM.
- ☐ Inertia force (D'Alembert) is **$-m\ddot{u}$** — always with the minus sign.
- ☐ Units in every final numerical answer.
- ☐ Show units in the EOM check: kg, N·s/m, N/m, N — all consistent.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Influence of Gravitational Forces, Generalized SDOF Systems, Solution of the Equation of Motion, Undamped Free Vibration, Damped Free Vibration, Critically Damped, Under Damped and Over Damped SDOF Systems, Logarithmic Decrement
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECET741 - STRUCTURAL DYNAMICS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Influence of Gravitational Forces, Generalized SDOF Systems, Solution of the Equation of Motion, Undamped Free Vibration, Damped Free Vibration, Critically Damped, Under Damped and Over Damped SDOF Systems, Logarithmic Decrement

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Structural Dynamics and the Single Degree of Freedom (SDOF) System

**Structural Dynamics** is the branch of structural engineering that studies the response of structures subjected to dynamic (time-varying) loads such as wind, earthquakes, blast, machine vibrations, and human-induced loads. A **Single Degree of Freedom (SDOF) system** is the most fundamental idealisation in which the entire mass of the structure is lumped at a single location and the system motion can be completely described by a single independent coordinate.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Definition (PECET741, Module 1):**
> An SDOF system is an idealised physical system in which only one independent coordinate is required to completely specify the configuration of the system at any instant of time. It is characterised by three basic properties: **mass (m)**, **stiffness (k)**, and **damping (c)**.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Real-World Intuition

Imagine a simple **pencil eraser placed on a stretched spring** held vertically on a table.

- If you pull the eraser down a little and release, it bounces up and down rhythmically — this is a **vibrating SDOF system**.
- The mass of the eraser is the **lumped mass (m)**.
- The spring stiffness is the **stiffness (k)**.
- The slight air resistance and internal friction of the spring that gradually stops the motion is the **damping (c)**.
- The force of gravity acting on the eraser is balanced by the static stretch of the spring and does **not** affect the dynamic vibration — this is the **influence of gravitational forces**.

In real engineering, this same analogy applies to:
- A **building** swaying under wind load (lumped mass = total floor mass, stiffness = lateral stiffness of columns).
- A **water tank** on a framed staging during an earthquake.
- A **machine foundation** experiencing rotating imbalance.
- A **bridge deck** under moving vehicle loads.

### 1.3 The Three Core Elements of an SDOF System

> [!NOTE]
> Every SDOF system in structural dynamics is fully defined by three idealised components:

1. **Mass (m):** Stores kinetic energy, resists acceleration by virtue of inertia. Units: **kg** or **kN·s²/m**.
2. **Stiffness (k):** Stores potential energy, resists displacement. Units: **N/m** or **kN/m**.
3. **Damping (c):** Dissipates energy, resists velocity. Units: **N·s/m** or **kN·s/m**.

### 1.4 The Equation of Motion — A Starting Point

The dynamic equilibrium of a mass–spring–damper system subjected to an external time-varying force $F(t)$ is expressed as:

$$
m\ddot{x}(t) + c\dot{x}(t) + kx(t) = F(t)
$$

where:
- $x(t)$ = dynamic displacement measured from the **static equilibrium position**
- $\dot{x}(t)$ = velocity
- $\ddot{x}(t)$ = acceleration
- $F(t)$ = applied dynamic force

When $F(t) = 0$, the system undergoes **free vibration** — the central topic of this module.

### 1.5 Influence of Gravitational Forces

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Concept (Board Exam Favourite):**
> Gravity does **not** appear in the dynamic equation of motion when the displacement coordinate is measured from the **static equilibrium position**. The gravity-induced static displacement is automatically absorbed into the equilibrium reference.

Let the total vertical displacement be $u(t)$ measured from the **unstretched** position of the spring. Then:

$$
m\ddot{u} + c\dot{u} + k(u - u_{st}) = -mg
$$

where $u_{st} = mg/k$ is the static deflection due to self-weight. The static equilibrium condition $ku_{st} = mg$ cancels the gravity term, leaving:

$$
m\ddot{x} + c\dot{x} + kx = F(t)
$$

with $x(t) = u(t) - u_{st}$. Therefore, **gravity influences only the static reference, not the dynamic response**.

### 1.6 Generalised SDOF Systems

A **generalised SDOF system** is one in which the entire rigid body or deformable system can be described by a single generalised coordinate $q(t)$ through an assumed displacement shape $\psi(x)$.

$$
u(x,t) = \psi(x) \cdot q(t)
$$

The equation of motion is derived using the **Lagrangian or D'Alembert principle** as:

$$
M^*\ddot{q}(t) + C^*\dot{q}(t) + K^*q(t) = F^*(t)
$$

where $M^*$, $C^*$, $K^*$ are the **generalised (effective) mass, damping, and stiffness** computed as integrals of the assumed shape function.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Classification of SDOF Vibration Regimes

The behaviour of a free-vibrating SDOF system depends entirely on the magnitude of the **damping ratio (ζ)**:

| Regime | Condition | Behaviour |
|--------|-----------|-----------|
| Undamped | $\zeta = 0$ | Pure sinusoidal oscillation, never decays |
| Underdamped | $0 < \zeta < 1$ | Decaying sinusoidal oscillation |
| Critically Damped | $\zeta = 1$ | Fastest non-oscillatory return to equilibrium |
| Overdamped | $\zeta > 1$ | Slow non-oscillatory return to equilibrium |

### 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Module 1)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table contains all **must-know formulas** for the KTU 2024 Scheme end-semester examination on Module 1 of Structural Dynamics.

| S.No. | Concept | Formula | Units / Notes |
|-------|---------|---------|----------------|
| 1 | Static deflection due to gravity | $u_{st} = \dfrac{mg}{k}$ | metres (m) |
| 2 | Unnatural frequency (natural circular frequency) | $\omega_n = \sqrt{\dfrac{k}{m}}$ | rad/s |
| 3 | Natural frequency in Hz | $f_n = \dfrac{\omega_n}{2\pi} = \dfrac{1}{T_n}$ | Hz |
| 4 | Natural time period | $T_n = 2\pi \sqrt{\dfrac{m}{k}}$ | seconds (s) |
| 5 | Critical damping coefficient | $c_{cr} = 2m\omega_n = 2\sqrt{km}$ | N·s/m |
| 6 | Damping ratio | $\zeta = \dfrac{c}{c_{cr}} = \dfrac{c}{2\sqrt{km}}$ | dimensionless |
| 7 | Damped natural frequency | $\omega_d = \omega_n \sqrt{1 - \zeta^2}$ | rad/s |
| 8 | Damped time period | $T_d = \dfrac{2\pi}{\omega_d} = \dfrac{T_n}{\sqrt{1-\zeta^2}}$ | seconds |
| 9 | Undamped free vibration solution | $x(t) = A\cos\omega_n t + B\sin\omega_n t$ | — |
| 10 | Equivalent amplitude–phase form | $x(t) = C\cos(\omega_n t - \phi)$ | $C = \sqrt{A^2+B^2}$ |
| 11 | Underdamped solution | $x(t) = e^{-\zeta\omega_n t}\left(A\cos\omega_d t + B\sin\omega_d t\right)$ | decays with time |
| 12 | Overdamped solution | $x(t) = A e^{s_1 t} + B e^{s_2 t}$ | $s_{1,2} = -\zeta\omega_n \pm \omega_n\sqrt{\zeta^2 - 1}$ |
| 13 | Critically damped solution | $x(t) = (A + Bt)e^{-\omega_n t}$ | non-oscillatory |
| 14 | Logarithmic decrement | $\delta = \dfrac{1}{n}\ln\left(\dfrac{x_0}{x_n}\right) = \dfrac{2\pi\zeta}{\sqrt{1-\zeta^2}}$ | dimensionless |
| 15 | Damping ratio from log decrement | $\zeta = \dfrac{\delta}{\sqrt{4\pi^2 + \delta^2}}$ | dimensionless |
| 16 | Generalised mass | $M^* = \displaystyle\int_0^L m(x)\psi^2(x)\,dx$ | kg |
| 17 | Generalised stiffness | $K^* = \displaystyle\int_0^L EI(x)\left[\psi''(x)\right]^2 dx$ | N/m |

### 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Seismic design of buildings:** The natural period $T_n$ determines resonance with earthquake ground motion.
- **Machine foundation design:** Operating frequency must be kept away from $\omega_n$ to prevent resonance failures.
- **Blast and impact analysis:** Understanding underdamped response helps size damping devices.
- **Bridge cable-stayed design:** Critical damping ratio of stay cables must be controlled using dampers to suppress rain–wind vibration.
- **Tuned Mass Dampers (TMD):** A small auxiliary SDOF oscillator attached to tall buildings (e.g., Taipei 101) uses precisely the equations above to suppress sway.

### 2.4 Key Conceptual Points to Internalise

- The **equation of motion is a second-order linear ordinary differential equation (ODE)** with constant coefficients.
- The **characteristic equation** controls the entire physics: real, distinct, equal, or complex conjugate roots.
- **Logarithmic decrement is a practical experimental tool** — engineers measure successive peak amplitudes in a free-vibration test and back-calculate the damping ratio without ever knowing the damping coefficient directly.
- **Gravity is dynamically invisible** when displacement is measured from the static equilibrium position. This is a major trick question in KTU papers.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the Equation of Motion Including Gravity

**Step 1 — Set up the free-body diagram.**
Consider a mass $m$ attached to a spring of stiffness $k$ and dashpot of damping $c$, hanging vertically. The applied dynamic force on the mass is $F(t)$ (positive upward), gravity is $-mg$, spring force is $-k(u - u_{st})$ if $u$ is measured from unstretched length.

**Step 2 — Apply Newton's second law** (positive direction = downward, displacement $u$ measured from unstretched position):

$$
m\ddot{u} = -k(u - u_{st}) - c\dot{u} + F(t) - mg
$$

**Step 3 — Apply static equilibrium condition.**
At static equilibrium, the velocity and acceleration are zero, and the only forces are gravity and the static spring stretch:

$$
0 = -k u_{st} - 0 + 0 - mg \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; k u_{st} = mg
$$

**Step 4 — Substitute** $u_{st} = mg/k$ into the dynamic equation:

$$
m\ddot{u} + c\dot{u} + k(u - u_{st}) = F(t) - mg
$$

Since $k u_{st} = mg$, these cancel:

$$
m\ddot{u} + c\dot{u} + k u = F(t) + k u_{st} - mg
$$

**Step 5 — Define dynamic displacement** $x(t) = u(t) - u_{st}$. Then $\dot{x} = \dot{u}$, $\ddot{x} = \ddot{u}$, and the equation becomes:

$$
\boxed{m\ddot{x}(t) + c\dot{x}(t) + kx(t) = F(t)}
$$

> **Conclusion:** The equation of motion is identical to the gravity-free case, but $x(t)$ is now measured from the static equilibrium position, **not** the geometric origin.

---

### 3.2 Solution of the Equation of Motion (Free Vibration, F(t) = 0)

Setting $F(t) = 0$:

$$
m\ddot{x} + c\dot{x} + kx = 0
$$

Dividing by $m$:

$$
\ddot{x} + 2\zeta\omega_n \dot{x} + \omega_n^2 x = 0
$$

where we have used the substitutions:

$$
\omega_n = \sqrt{\dfrac{k}{m}}, \qquad 2\zeta\omega_n = \dfrac{c}{m}
$$

Assume a solution of the form $x(t) = e^{st}$. Substituting:

$$
s^2 e^{st} + 2\zeta\omega_n s\, e^{st} + \omega_n^2 e^{st} = 0
$$

Dividing by $e^{st}$ (which is never zero):

$$
s^2 + 2\zeta\omega_n s + \omega_n^2 = 0
$$

This is the **characteristic equation**. Solving using the quadratic formula:

$$
s = -\zeta\omega_n \pm \omega_n\sqrt{\zeta^2 - 1}
$$

The form of the solution depends entirely on the discriminant $\Delta = \zeta^2 - 1$.

---

### 3.3 Case 1: Undamped Free Vibration (ζ = 0, c = 0)

The characteristic equation reduces to $s^2 + \omega_n^2 = 0$, giving imaginary roots $s = \pm i\omega_n$.

The general solution is:

$$
x(t) = A\cos(\omega_n t) + B\sin(\omega_n t)
$$

Applying initial conditions $x(0) = x_0$ and $\dot{x}(0) = v_0$:

- At $t=0$: $x(0) = A = x_0$
- Differentiating: $\dot{x}(t) = -A\omega_n \sin\omega_n t + B\omega_n \cos\omega_n t$
- At $t=0$: $\dot{x}(0) = B\omega_n = v_0 \;\Rightarrow\; B = v_0/\omega_n$

Therefore:

$$
\boxed{x(t) = x_0 \cos(\omega_n t) + \dfrac{v_0}{\omega_n}\sin(\omega_n t)}
$$

In amplitude–phase form:

$$
x(t) = C\cos(\omega_n t - \phi)
$$

where $C = \sqrt{x_0^2 + (v_0/\omega_n)^2}$ and $\tan\phi = v_0/(x_0 \omega_n)$.

**Key physical insight:** An undamped SDOF system oscillates **forever** with constant amplitude $C$ and period $T_n = 2\pi/\omega_n$. This is an idealisation — in reality, all physical systems have at least some damping.

---

### 3.4 Case 2: Underdamped Free Vibration (0 < ζ < 1)

The discriminant $\zeta^2 - 1 < 0$, so we have two **complex conjugate roots**:

$$
s_{1,2} = -\zeta\omega_n \pm i\omega_n\sqrt{1 - \zeta^2} = -\zeta\omega_n \pm i\omega_d
$$

where $\omega_d = \omega_n\sqrt{1 - \zeta^2}$ is the **damped natural frequency**.

The general solution is:

$$
x(t) = e^{-\zeta\omega_n t}\left(A\cos\omega_d t + B\sin\omega_d t\right)
$$

Apply initial conditions $x(0)=x_0$ and $\dot{x}(0)=v_0$:

- $x(0) = A = x_0$
- $\dot{x}(0) = -\zeta\omega_n A + B\omega_d = v_0$
- $\Rightarrow B = \dfrac{v_0 + \zeta\omega_n x_0}{\omega_d}$

Therefore:

$$
\boxed{x(t) = e^{-\zeta\omega_n t}\left[x_0 \cos\omega_d t + \dfrac{v_0 + \zeta\omega_n x_0}{\omega_d}\sin\omega_d t\right]}
$$

In amplitude–phase form:

$$
x(t) = C\, e^{-\zeta\omega_n t}\cos(\omega_d t - \phi)
$$

where $C = \sqrt{x_0^2 + \left(\dfrac{v_0 + \zeta\omega_n x_0}{\omega_d}\right)^2}$.

**Key physical insight:** The oscillation amplitude decays exponentially with time constant $1/(\zeta\omega_n)$. The frequency of oscillation is **slightly lower** than the undamped natural frequency.

---

### 3.5 Case 3: Critically Damped Free Vibration (ζ = 1, c = ccr)

The discriminant $\zeta^2 - 1 = 0$, giving a **repeated real root** $s = -\omega_n$.

The general solution takes the special form (because the two independent solutions coalesce into one):

$$
\boxed{x(t) = (A + Bt)\,e^{-\omega_n t}}
$$

Apply initial conditions:

- $x(0) = A = x_0$
- $\dot{x}(t) = Be^{-\omega_n t} - \omega_n(A + Bt)e^{-\omega_n t}$
- $\dot{x}(0) = B - \omega_n A = v_0 \;\Rightarrow\; B = v_0 + \omega_n x_0$

Therefore:

$$
x(t) = \left[x_0 + (v_0 + \omega_n x_0)t\right]e^{-\omega_n t}
$$

**Key physical insight:** Critical damping is the **smallest amount of damping that prevents any oscillation**. The system returns to equilibrium in the **shortest possible time** without overshooting. This is the design target for many engineering systems (e.g., door closers, vehicle suspension systems, analog measuring instruments).

---

### 3.6 Case 4: Overdamped Free Vibration (ζ > 1, c > ccr)

The discriminant $\zeta^2 - 1 > 0$, giving two **distinct negative real roots**:

$$
s_1 = -\zeta\omega_n + \omega_n\sqrt{\zeta^2 - 1}, \qquad s_2 = -\zeta\omega_n - \omega_n\sqrt{\zeta^2 - 1}
$$

Both roots are negative (assuming $\zeta > 1$), so the solution is:

$$
\boxed{x(t) = A e^{s_1 t} + B e^{s_2 t}}
$$

Apply initial conditions:

- $x(0) = A + B = x_0$
- $\dot{x}(0) = A s_1 + B s_2 = v_0$

Solving:

$$
A = \dfrac{v_0 - s_2 x_0}{s_1 - s_2}, \qquad B = \dfrac{s_1 x_0 - v_0}{s_1 - s_2}
$$

**Key physical insight:** Overdamped systems return to equilibrium **without oscillation**, but **slower** than critically damped systems. The two exponentials correspond to a "fast" and a "slow" mode.

---

### 3.7 Derivation of the Logarithmic Decrement

The logarithmic decrement is the **most important practical tool** for experimentally determining the damping ratio in a free-vibration test.

**Step 1 — Start with the underdamped amplitude envelope.**
The peak amplitudes occur at times $t_n = 2\pi n/\omega_d$ (where $n$ is a positive integer). At each peak, the cosine term equals 1 (or $-1$, taking the envelope value), so the peak amplitude is:

$$
x_n = C\,e^{-\zeta\omega_n t_n} = C\,e^{-\zeta\omega_n (2\pi n/\omega_d)}
$$

**Step 2 — Form the ratio of successive peaks:**

$$
\dfrac{x_n}{x_{n+1}} = \dfrac{C e^{-\zeta\omega_n (2\pi n/\omega_d)}}{C e^{-\zeta\omega_n (2\pi(n+1)/\omega_d)}} = e^{\zeta\omega_n (2\pi/\omega_d)}
$$

**Step 3 — Take natural logarithm** to define the **logarithmic decrement**:

$$
\delta = \ln\left(\dfrac{x_n}{x_{n+1}}\right) = \dfrac{2\pi\zeta\omega_n}{\omega_d}
$$

**Step 4 — Substitute** $\omega_d = \omega_n\sqrt{1 - \zeta^2}$:

$$
\boxed{\delta = \dfrac{2\pi\zeta}{\sqrt{1 - \zeta^2}}}
$$

**Step 5 — Invert to obtain the damping ratio:**

$$
\delta^2 (1 - \zeta^2) = 4\pi^2 \zeta^2
$$

$$
\delta^2 = \zeta^2 (4\pi^2 + \delta^2)
$$

$$
\boxed{\zeta = \dfrac{\delta}{\sqrt{4\pi^2 + \delta^2}}}
$$

**Step 6 — For small damping (ζ < 0.2), the approximation** $\delta \approx 2\pi\zeta$ is excellent and is widely used.

**Practical lab formula** (using $n$ cycles):

$$
\delta = \dfrac{1}{n}\ln\left(\dfrac{x_0}{x_n}\right)
$$

This is the form used most often in undergraduate dynamics labs.

---

### 3.8 Generalised SDOF System — Rayleigh's Method

For a system with distributed mass and stiffness described by a single generalised coordinate $q(t)$ and shape function $\psi(x)$:

$$
u(x,t) = \psi(x) \cdot q(t)
$$

**Kinetic Energy:**

$$
T = \dfrac{1}{2} \int_0^L m(x) \left[\dot{u}(x,t)\right]^2 dx = \dfrac{1}{2} \dot{q}^2 \int_0^L m(x) \psi^2(x)\,dx
$$

**Strain Energy:**

$$
V = \dfrac{1}{2} \int_0^L EI(x) \left[\psi''(x)\right]^2 dx \cdot q^2
$$

**Applying Lagrange's equation** $\dfrac{d}{dt}\left(\dfrac{\partial T}{\partial \dot q}\right) + \dfrac{\partial V}{\partial q} = Q^*$:

$$
\left[\int_0^L m(x)\psi^2\,dx\right]\ddot{q} + \left[\int_0^L EI(\psi'')^2\,dx\right]q = Q^*(t)
$$

$$
\boxed{M^* \ddot q + K^* q = F^*(t)}
$$

where $M^*$ and $K^*$ are the **generalised mass** and **generalised stiffness**.

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
**Define the damping ratio and the critical damping coefficient. Explain the physical significance of critical damping in SDOF systems.**

**Model Answer:**

The **damping ratio** $\zeta$ is defined as the ratio of the actual damping coefficient to the critical damping coefficient:

$$
\zeta = \dfrac{c}{c_{cr}}
$$

The **critical damping coefficient** is the minimum value of damping required to prevent any oscillation in a free-vibrating SDOF system:

$$
c_{cr} = 2m\omega_n = 2\sqrt{km}
$$

**Physical significance:** A critically damped system ($\zeta = 1$) returns to its equilibrium position in the **shortest possible time without oscillating or overshooting**. It represents the boundary between oscillatory (underdamped) and non-oscillatory (overdamped) behaviour. In engineering practice (e.g., galvanometers, door closers, vehicle suspensions), critical damping is the design ideal because it combines rapid response with stability.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]**
**Explain the concept of generalised SDOF system. How is the effective mass and stiffness computed for a cantilever beam using a generalised coordinate?**

**Model Answer:**

A **generalised SDOF system** is one in which the actual continuous or multi-DOF system is idealised so that its motion can be described by a single **generalised coordinate** $q(t)$ through an assumed shape function $\psi(x)$. The displacement field is written as $u(x,t) = \psi(x) \cdot q(t)$.

For a cantilever beam of length $L$, flexural rigidity $EI$, and distributed mass per unit length $\bar{m}$, the **effective mass** and **effective stiffness** are:

$$
M^* = \int_0^L \bar{m}(x)\,\psi^2(x)\,dx
$$

$$
K^* = \int_0^L EI(x)\left[\psi''(x)\right]^2 dx
$$

Using the static deflection shape $\psi(x) = 1 - \cos(\pi x / 2L)$ as the assumed mode, the resulting equation of motion is:

$$
M^* \ddot q + K^* q = F^*(t)
$$

This yields an approximate natural frequency of the beam with reasonable accuracy for the first mode.

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — Dec 2022]**

**(a)** Derive the equation of motion of a SDOF system subjected to a vertical dynamic force. Show clearly how the gravitational force gets eliminated when displacement is measured from the static equilibrium position. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** An industrial chimney of mass $m = 5000$ kg and lateral stiffness $k = 2 \times 10^6$ N/m is subjected to a horizontal impulsive force causing an initial displacement of $x_0 = 0.02$ m and initial velocity $v_0 = 0.5$ m/s. The system has negligible damping. Compute: (i) the natural frequency, (ii) the natural time period, and (iii) the maximum dynamic displacement of the chimney. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution to Part (a):**

Refer to the detailed derivation in Section 3.1 above. Key points to write in the answer script:

1. Draw a free-body diagram of the mass $m$ hanging from spring $k$ with gravity acting downward.
2. Apply Newton's second law: $m\ddot{u} = -k(u - u_{st}) - c\dot{u} + F(t) - mg$.
3. Static equilibrium gives $k u_{st} = mg$, so gravity-induced terms cancel.
4. Define $x(t) = u(t) - u_{st}$ and obtain $m\ddot{x} + c\dot{x} + kx = F(t)$.

**Model Solution to Part (b):**

**Given:** $m = 5000$ kg, $k = 2 \times 10^6$ N/m, $x_0 = 0.02$ m, $v_0 = 0.5$ m/s, $c = 0$.

**(i) Natural frequency:**

$$
\omega_n = \sqrt{\dfrac{k}{m}} = \sqrt{\dfrac{2 \times 10^6}{5000}} = \sqrt{400} = 20 \text{ rad/s}
$$

**(ii) Natural time period:**

$$
T_n = \dfrac{2\pi}{\omega_n} = \dfrac{2\pi}{20} = 0.3142 \text{ s}
$$

**(iii) Maximum dynamic displacement:**

For undamped free vibration:

$$
x(t) = x_0 \cos(\omega_n t) + \dfrac{v_0}{\omega_n}\sin(\omega_n t)
$$

The amplitude is:

$$
C = \sqrt{x_0^2 + \left(\dfrac{v_0}{\omega_n}\right)^2} = \sqrt{(0.02)^2 + \left(\dfrac{0.5}{20}\right)^2}
$$

$$
C = \sqrt{0.0004 + 0.000625} = \sqrt{0.001025} = 0.03202 \text{ m}
$$

**Maximum dynamic displacement = 32.02 mm.**

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — May 2023]**

**(a)** A SDOF system has $m = 100$ kg, $k = 4 \times 10^4$ N/m, and $c = 400$ N·s/m. Classify the damping regime and obtain the complete free-vibration response if $x(0) = 0.01$ m and $\dot{x}(0) = 0.1$ m/s. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Define logarithmic decrement. In a free-vibration test on a structure, the amplitude reduces from 20 mm to 2 mm in 8 cycles. Compute the damping ratio and the damping coefficient if the mass is $m = 500$ kg and stiffness is $k = 5 \times 10^5$ N/m. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution to Part (a):**

**Given:** $m = 100$ kg, $k = 4 \times 10^4$ N/m, $c = 400$ N·s/m, $x_0 = 0.01$ m, $v_0 = 0.1$ m/s.

**Step 1 — Natural frequency:**

$$
\omega_n = \sqrt{\dfrac{4 \times 10^4}{100}} = \sqrt{400} = 20 \text{ rad/s}
$$

**Step 2 — Critical damping:**

$$
c_{cr} = 2m\omega_n = 2 \times 100 \times 20 = 4000 \text{ N·s/m}
$$

**Step 3 — Damping ratio:**

$$
\zeta = \dfrac{c}{c_{cr}} = \dfrac{400}{4000} = 0.1
$$

Since $0 < \zeta < 1$, the system is **underdamped**.

**Step 4 — Damped natural frequency:**

$$
\omega_d = \omega_n\sqrt{1 - \zeta^2} = 20\sqrt{1 - 0.01} = 20 \times 0.995 = 19.90 \text{ rad/s}
$$

**Step 5 — General solution:**

$$
x(t) = e^{-\zeta\omega_n t}\left(A\cos\omega_d t + B\sin\omega_d t\right)
$$

**Step 6 — Apply initial conditions:**

$x(0) = A = 0.01$ m.

$\dot{x}(0) = -\zeta\omega_n A + B\omega_d = v_0$:

$$
B = \dfrac{v_0 + \zeta\omega_n x_0}{\omega_d} = \dfrac{0.1 + 0.1 \times 20 \times 0.01}{19.90} = \dfrac{0.1 + 0.02}{19.90} = \dfrac{0.12}{19.90} = 0.00603 \text{ m}
$$

**Final response:**

$$
\boxed{x(t) = e^{-2t}\left[0.01\cos(19.90\,t) + 0.00603\sin(19.90\,t)\right] \text{ m}}
$$

**Model Solution to Part (b):**

**Given:** $x_0 = 20$ mm, $x_n = 2$ mm at $n = 8$ cycles, $m = 500$ kg, $k = 5 \times 10^5$ N/m.

**Logarithmic Decrement Definition:**
The logarithmic decrement $\delta$ is the natural logarithm of the ratio of successive peak amplitudes in a free-vibration response. It is a direct measure of the energy dissipated per cycle of oscillation.

$$
\delta = \dfrac{1}{n}\ln\left(\dfrac{x_0}{x_n}\right)
$$

**Step 1 — Compute $\delta$:**

$$
\delta = \dfrac{1}{8}\ln\left(\dfrac{20}{2}\right) = \dfrac{1}{8}\ln(10) = \dfrac{2.3026}{8} = 0.2878
$$

**Step 2 — Damping ratio:**

$$
\zeta = \dfrac{\delta}{\sqrt{4\pi^2 + \delta^2}} = \dfrac{0.2878}{\sqrt{39.478 + 0.0828}} = \dfrac{0.2878}{\sqrt{39.561}} = \dfrac{0.2878}{6.290} = 0.04576
$$

**Step 3 — Damping coefficient:**

First compute $\omega_n$:

$$
\omega_n = \sqrt{\dfrac{k}{m}} = \sqrt{\dfrac{5 \times 10^5}{500}} = \sqrt{1000} = 31.623 \text{ rad/s}
$$

$$
c = \zeta \cdot 2m\omega_n = 0.04576 \times 2 \times 500 \times 31.623 = 1447.3 \text{ N·s/m}
$$

**Final answers:** $\delta = 0.2878$, $\zeta = 0.0458$, $c = 1447$ N·s/m.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Gravity trap:** Many students include $-mg$ in the equation of motion and lose **2–3 marks**. Always measure $x(t)$ from the static equilibrium position, or explicitly show that gravity cancels.
> 2. **Wrong critical damping formula:** Writing $c_{cr} = \sqrt{km}$ instead of $c_{cr} = 2\sqrt{km}$ is a **frequently repeated error** costing **2 marks** every year.
> 3. **Logarithmic decrement base:** Use **natural logarithm (ln)**, not log base 10. Many problems in KTU papers specify the base — read carefully.
> 4. **Sign of initial velocity:** A positive initial velocity conventionally means motion in the positive $x$ direction. Take care with sign conventions.
> 5. **Damped vs undamped natural frequency:** The damped frequency $\omega_d < \omega_n$ **always** for underdamped systems. Never substitute $\omega_n$ for $\omega_d$ in the solution.
> 6. **Classification trap:** A problem with $c > c_{cr}$ does **not** mean overdamped if the values are not consistent — always recompute $\zeta$ and verify.
> 7. **Units consistency:** $k$ must be in N/m (not kN/m) when $m$ is in kg. Convert kN/m to N/m by multiplying by 1000 before using $c_{cr} = 2\sqrt{km}$.

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Module 1, PECET741:**

- ☐ An **SDOF system** has only **one independent coordinate** to describe its motion at any time.
- ☐ The **three fundamental properties** are mass $m$, stiffness $k$, and damping $c$.
- ☐ The **equation of motion** is $m\ddot{x} + c\dot{x} + kx = F(t)$, with $x$ measured from **static equilibrium position**.
- ☐ **Gravity** is automatically absorbed into the static reference and **does not appear** in the dynamic equation.
- ☐ **Natural circular frequency** is $\omega_n = \sqrt{k/m}$ in rad/s.
- ☐ **Natural period** is $T_n = 2\pi\sqrt{m/k}$ in seconds.
- ☐ **Critical damping** is $c_{cr} = 2m\omega_n = 2\sqrt{km}$.
- ☐ **Damping ratio** $\zeta = c / c_{cr}$ is dimensionless and **classifies the system**.
- ☐ **Undamped** ($\zeta=0$): $x(t) = A\cos\omega_n t + B\sin\omega_n t$.
- ☐ **Underdamped** ($0<\zeta<1$): decaying sinusoid with $\omega_d = \omega_n\sqrt{1-\zeta^2}$.
- ☐ **Critically damped** ($\zeta=1$): $x(t) = (A+Bt)e^{-\omega_n t}$, non-oscillatory, fastest return.
- ☐ **Overdamped** ($\zeta>1$): $x(t) = Ae^{s_1 t} + Be^{s_2 t}$, non-oscillatory, slow return.
- ☐ **Logarithmic decrement** $\delta = \ln(x_n/x_{n+1}) = 2\pi\zeta/\sqrt{1-\zeta^2}$.
- ☐ **Damping ratio from log decrement** $\zeta = \delta/\sqrt{4\pi^2 + \delta^2}$.
- ☐ **For small damping** ($\zeta < 0.2$): $\delta \approx 2\pi\zeta$.
- ☐ **Generalised SDOF** uses assumed shape $\psi(x)$ to reduce a continuous system to one coordinate $q(t)$.
- ☐ **Generalised mass** $M^* = \int m(x)\psi^2 dx$; **Generalised stiffness** $K^* = \int EI(\psi'')^2 dx$.
- ☐ **Energy dissipation per cycle** in a damped system is $\Delta E = \pi c \omega_d X^2$.
- ☐ **Resonance** occurs when forcing frequency $\omega \to \omega_n$ in undamped systems.
- ☐ **Quality factor** $Q = 1/(2\zeta)$ is used in vibration-based health monitoring of structures.
