> - **Course:** PECET741 - STRUCTURAL DYNAMICS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Response to Harmonic Loading, Steady State and Transient States, Steady State Amplitude, Dynamic Magnification Factor, Force Transmissibility and Vibration Isolation, Response to Periodic Loading, Fourier Series Representation of Periodic Loads in Time Domain, Response of SDOF Systems, Response to Impulse Loading, Half-sine, Rectangular and Triangular Pulses

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Response to Harmonic Loading — Definition

A **harmonic load** is a sinusoidal (or cosinusoidal) time-varying force applied to a structure. The general form of a harmonic excitation is:

$$F(t) = F_0 \sin(\omega t) \quad \text{or} \quad F(t) = F_0 \cos(\omega t)$$

where:
- $F_0$ = **amplitude** of the applied force (in N or kN)
- $\omega$ = **forcing frequency** (in rad/s)
- $t$ = time (in s)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The study of harmonic response is the **cornerstone of structural dynamics** because any periodic load can be expressed as a sum of harmonic components (Fourier Series). The system's response to a single harmonic load therefore reveals how it will respond to virtually any deterministic loading.

### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy: Pushing a Child on a Swing**
> Imagine you push a child on a swing at a steady rhythm — once every 2 seconds. That is a harmonic force. 
> - If you push **slowly** (low frequency), the swing gently sways with the push (in-phase response). 
> - If you push **at the swing's natural rhythm** (resonance), even a tiny push builds up enormous amplitude — the system amplifies your input! 
> - If you push **very fast** (high frequency), the swing barely moves because the inertia resists the rapid changes.
> 
> This is exactly how a building or machine behaves under a rotating machine's unbalanced force.

---

## 1.2 Steady State and Transient States

The total response of a **damped SDOF system** subjected to any arbitrary load consists of two superposed parts:

| State | Symbol | Origin | Duration |
|-------|--------|--------|----------|
| **Transient response** | $u_t(t)$ | Initial conditions (free vibration) + early-stage forced response | Dies out due to damping |
| **Steady-state response** | $u_{ss}(t)$ | Long-term forced response dictated by the input | Persists as long as load is applied |

Mathematically:

$$u(t) = u_t(t) + u_{ss}(t)$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Transient state** is the part that **decays with time** due to damping, while the **steady state** is the part that **persists indefinitely**. After sufficient time (typically $t > 4/\xi\omega_n$, or about 4–5 times the natural period), the transient part becomes negligible and the system is said to have reached its **steady-state** condition.

---

## 1.3 Steady State Amplitude

The **steady-state amplitude** $U$ is the maximum displacement the system reaches during its steady-state oscillation. For a harmonically excited SDOF system with damping:

$$U = \frac{u_{st}}{\sqrt{(1 - r^2)^2 + (2\xi r)^2}}$$

where:
- $u_{st} = F_0/k$ = static deflection
- $r = \omega/\omega_n$ = frequency ratio
- $\xi = c/(2m\omega_n)$ = damping ratio

---

## 1.4 Dynamic Magnification Factor (DMF)

The **Dynamic Magnification Factor** (also called Magnification Factor, $D$) is the ratio of steady-state dynamic amplitude to the static deflection:

$$\boxed{D = \frac{U}{u_{st}} = \frac{1}{\sqrt{(1 - r^2)^2 + (2\xi r)^2}}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> $D$ tells us **how much the dynamic displacement is amplified** (or reduced) compared to applying the same force statically. At resonance ($r=1$) and zero damping ($\xi=0$), $D \to \infty$ — this is the famous **resonance catastrophe**.

---

## 1.5 Force Transmissibility and Vibration Isolation

When a machine generates a harmonic force, some of this force is transmitted to its supporting structure. **Force Transmissibility (TR)** is defined as the ratio of the force transmitted to the support to the applied force:

$$TR = \frac{F_T}{F_0} = \sqrt{\frac{1 + (2\xi r)^2}{(1 - r^2)^2 + (2\xi r)^2}}$$

- If $TR < 1$, the system acts as a **vibration isolator** (most of the force is absorbed).
- If $TR > 1$, the system amplifies the transmitted force (undesirable).
- For $r > \sqrt{2}$, $TR < 1$ for **any damping value** — this is the **isolation region**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Vibration isolation** is achieved by ensuring the forcing frequency is **greater than $\sqrt{2}$ times the natural frequency** ($r > \sqrt{2}$). In this region, the isolator transmits less force than is applied.

---

## 1.6 Response to Periodic Loading

A **periodic load** repeats itself after a fixed time $T_p$:

$$F(t + T_p) = F(t)$$

The response of an SDOF system to periodic loading is built using the **Superposition Principle**: solve for each harmonic component separately and sum the responses.

---

## 1.7 Fourier Series Representation of Periodic Loads

Any periodic function $F(t)$ of period $T_p$ can be represented as a Fourier series:

$$F(t) = a_0 + \sum_{n=1}^{\infty}\left[a_n \cos(n\omega_p t) + b_n \sin(n\omega_p t)\right]$$

where $\omega_p = 2\pi/T_p$ is the fundamental frequency, and the coefficients are:

$$a_0 = \frac{1}{T_p}\int_0^{T_p} F(t)\,dt$$

$$a_n = \frac{2}{T_p}\int_0^{T_p} F(t)\cos(n\omega_p t)\,dt$$

$$b_n = \frac{2}{T_p}\int_0^{T_p} F(t)\sin(n\omega_p t)\,dt$$

---

## 1.8 Response to Impulse Loading

An **impulse** is a very large force acting over a very short duration. The ideal impulse is a Dirac delta function, $F(t) = \hat{F}\delta(t)$, where $\hat{F}$ is the impulse magnitude (units: N·s).

For an impulse applied to a SDOF undamped system, the resulting response is **free vibration** starting from the initial conditions:

$$u(0) = 0, \quad \dot{u}(0) = \frac{\hat{F}}{m}$$

The displacement is:

$$u(t) = \frac{\hat{F}}{m\omega_d}e^{-\xi\omega_n t}\sin(\omega_d t)$$

For **practical (non-ideal) impulses**, three common shapes are:
1. **Rectangular pulse** — constant force for duration $t_d$
2. **Half-sine pulse** — $F(t) = F_0 \sin(\pi t/t_d)$ for $0 \le t \le t_d$
3. **Triangular pulse** — linearly rising then falling force

### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy: Hammer hitting a nail**
> A hammer delivers an almost-instantaneous force (impulse) to a nail. The nail's response is not determined by the peak force of the hammer but by the **area under the force-time curve** (impulse magnitude). Similarly, an SDOF system's response to an impulse is essentially **free vibration** launched by the impulse.
> 
> The shape of the pulse (rectangular, triangular, half-sine) only matters for **short-duration** pulses where the duration $t_d$ is comparable to the natural period $T_n$.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Derivation of Steady-State Response to Harmonic Loading

Consider the equation of motion of a damped SDOF system under harmonic loading:

$$m\ddot{u} + c\dot{u} + ku = F_0 \sin(\omega t)$$

### Step 1: Propose a Steady-State Solution

Assume a particular solution of the form:

$$u_{ss}(t) = A\sin(\omega t) + B\cos(\omega t)$$

or equivalently $u_{ss}(t) = U\sin(\omega t - \phi)$, where $U$ is the amplitude and $\phi$ is the phase angle.

### Step 2: Substitute and Match Coefficients

Substituting and matching coefficients of $\sin(\omega t)$ and $\cos(\omega t)$:

$$A(k - m\omega^2) + B(c\omega) = F_0$$

$$-A(c\omega) + B(k - m\omega^2) = 0$$

### Step 3: Solve for Amplitude and Phase

Solving these simultaneously yields:

$$U = \frac{F_0}{\sqrt{(k - m\omega^2)^2 + (c\omega)^2}}$$

$$\tan\phi = \frac{c\omega}{k - m\omega^2}$$

In terms of standard dimensionless parameters ($u_{st} = F_0/k$, $r = \omega/\omega_n$, $\xi = c/(2m\omega_n)$):

$$U = \frac{u_{st}}{\sqrt{(1 - r^2)^2 + (2\xi r)^2}}$$

$$\phi = \tan^{-1}\left(\frac{2\xi r}{1 - r^2}\right)$$

> [!NOTE]
> The phase angle $\phi$ represents the **lag** of the response behind the applied force. At low frequencies ($r \to 0$), $\phi \to 0$ (in-phase). At resonance ($r = 1$), $\phi = 90°$. At very high frequencies ($r \to \infty$), $\phi \to 180°$ (anti-phase).

---

## 2.2 Response Phases Visualization

| Frequency Regime | $r$ Range | System Behavior | Phase Lag $\phi$ |
|------------------|-----------|-----------------|------------------|
| Quasi-static | $r \ll 1$ | $U \approx u_{st}$ | $\approx 0°$ |
| Resonance | $r = 1$ | Maximum amplification (for small $\xi$) | $90°$ |
| Inertia-controlled | $r \gg 1$ | $U \to 0$ | $\approx 180°$ |

---

## 2.3 Dynamic Magnification Factor — Key Properties

| Condition | $D$ Value | Interpretation |
|-----------|-----------|----------------|
| $r = 0$ (static) | $D = 1$ | No amplification |
| $r = 1$, $\xi = 0$ | $D = \infty$ | Resonance catastrophe |
| $r = 1$, $\xi > 0$ | $D = 1/(2\xi)$ | Finite peak at resonance |
| $r = \sqrt{2}$ | $D = 1$ (any $\xi$) | Cross-over point |
| $r \to \infty$ | $D \to 0$ | Mass-controlled, no displacement |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The frequency $r = \sqrt{2}$ is special: the **DMF equals 1 regardless of damping**. For $r > \sqrt{2}$, $D < 1$ always — meaning the dynamic response is **smaller than the static response**. This is the basis of **vibration isolation by stiffness design**.

---

## 2.4 Force Transmissibility — Derivation

The force transmitted to the support is the sum of the spring force and damper force (assuming base is fixed):

$$F_T(t) = ku(t) + c\dot{u}(t)$$

For harmonic steady state, the amplitude of the transmitted force is:

$$F_T = \sqrt{(kU)^2 + (c\omega U)^2} = kU\sqrt{1 + (2\xi r)^2}$$

Dividing by the applied force amplitude $F_0$:

$$TR = \frac{F_T}{F_0} = \sqrt{\frac{1 + (2\xi r)^2}{(1 - r^2)^2 + (2\xi r)^2}}$$

| Frequency Regime | Transmissibility Behavior |
|------------------|---------------------------|
| $r \to 0$ | $TR \to 1$ (all force transmitted) |
| $r = 1$ | $TR = \sqrt{(1 + 4\xi^2)/(4\xi^2)} \ge 1$ (amplified) |
| $r = \sqrt{2}$ | $TR = 1$ (cross-over) |
| $r > \sqrt{2}$ | $TR < 1$ (isolation) |

> [!NOTE]
> Counterintuitively, **adding more damping increases transmissibility at high frequencies** ($r > \sqrt{2}$). Therefore, isolation system designers prefer **light damping** (e.g., rubber mounts with $\xi \approx 0.05$) for best isolation.

---

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---------|-------------|-------|
| 1 | $F(t) = F_0\sin(\omega t)$ | Harmonic force | N |
| 2 | $u_{st} = F_0/k$ | Static deflection | m |
| 3 | $r = \omega/\omega_n$ | Frequency ratio | dimensionless |
| 4 | $\xi = c/(2m\omega_n)$ | Damping ratio | dimensionless |
| 5 | $U = u_{st}/\sqrt{(1 - r^2)^2 + (2\xi r)^2}$ | Steady-state amplitude | m |
| 6 | $D = 1/\sqrt{(1 - r^2)^2 + (2\xi r)^2}$ | Dynamic magnification factor | dimensionless |
| 7 | $\phi = \tan^{-1}(2\xi r/(1 - r^2))$ | Phase angle | rad |
| 8 | $TR = \sqrt{(1 + (2\xi r)^2)/[(1 - r^2)^2 + (2\xi r)^2]}$ | Transmissibility | dimensionless |
| 9 | $u(t) = u_t(t) + u_{ss}(t)$ | Total response decomposition | m |
| 10 | $u_t(t) = e^{-\xi\omega_n t}[A\cos\omega_d t + B\sin\omega_d t]$ | Transient response | m |
| 11 | $F(t) = a_0 + \sum[a_n\cos(n\omega_p t) + b_n\sin(n\omega_p t)]$ | Fourier series | N |
| 12 | $\omega_p = 2\pi/T_p$ | Fundamental frequency | rad/s |
| 13 | $u(t) = (\hat{F}/m\omega_d)e^{-\xi\omega_n t}\sin(\omega_d t)$ | Response to ideal impulse | m |

---

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Concept | Real-World Application |
|---------|------------------------|
| Harmonic response | Design of buildings for wind-induced vortex shedding, design of bridges for pedestrian loading |
| Dynamic Magnification Factor | Evaluating whether a machine will operate safely near resonance |
| Transmissibility | Designing rubber mounts for HVAC units, engines, sensitive equipment |
| Vibration isolation | Floor isolation in hospitals for MRI machines, precision instrument labs |
| Fourier series | Earthquake response spectrum analysis, wind load decomposition |
| Impulse response | Blast loading on structures, pile-driving analysis, vehicle crash analysis |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Complete Derivation: Steady-State Response of Damped SDOF to Harmonic Force

### Given:
$$m\ddot{u} + c\dot{u} + ku = F_0 \sin(\omega t)$$

### Step 1: Identify Standard Form Parameters

We define the following dimensionless parameters to simplify analysis:

- Natural frequency: $\omega_n = \sqrt{k/m}$ (rad/s)
- Static deflection: $u_{st} = F_0/k$ (m)
- Damping ratio: $\xi = c/(2m\omega_n)$ (dimensionless)
- Frequency ratio: $r = \omega/\omega_n$ (dimensionless)

Dividing the entire equation of motion by $k$:

$$\frac{m}{k}\ddot{u} + \frac{c}{k}\dot{u} + u = u_{st}\sin(\omega t)$$

### Step 2: Propose Steady-State Solution

We assume the steady-state response is a harmonic function with the same frequency as the forcing function but with a phase lag:

$$u_{ss}(t) = U \sin(\omega t - \phi)$$

Expanding using the trigonometric identity:

$$u_{ss}(t) = U \cos(\phi) \sin(\omega t) - U \sin(\phi) \cos(\omega t)$$

Let $A = U\cos\phi$ and $B = -U\sin\phi$:

$$u_{ss}(t) = A \sin(\omega t) + B \cos(\omega t)$$

### Step 3: Compute Derivatives

$$\dot{u}_{ss}(t) = A \omega \cos(\omega t) - B \omega \sin(\omega t)$$

$$\ddot{u}_{ss}(t) = -A \omega^2 \sin(\omega t) - B \omega^2 \cos(\omega t)$$

### Step 4: Substitute into Equation of Motion

$$m(-A\omega^2 \sin\omega t - B\omega^2 \cos\omega t) + c(A\omega \cos\omega t - B\omega \sin\omega t) + k(A \sin\omega t + B \cos\omega t) = F_0 \sin\omega t$$

### Step 5: Collect $\sin(\omega t)$ and $\cos(\omega t)$ Terms

**Coefficient of $\sin(\omega t)$:**

$$-mA\omega^2 - cB\omega + kA = F_0$$

$$A(k - m\omega^2) - cB\omega = F_0$$

**Coefficient of $\cos(\omega t)$:**

$$-mB\omega^2 + cA\omega + kB = 0$$

$$A(c\omega) + B(k - m\omega^2) = 0$$

### Step 6: Solve the 2x2 System

From the second equation:

$$A = -\frac{B(k - m\omega^2)}{c\omega}$$

Substitute into the first equation:

$$-\frac{B(k - m\omega^2)^2}{c\omega} - cB\omega = F_0$$

$$B\left[-\frac{(k - m\omega^2)^2 + (c\omega)^2}{c\omega}\right] = F_0$$

$$B = -\frac{F_0 c\omega}{(k - m\omega^2)^2 + (c\omega)^2}$$

Similarly:

$$A = \frac{F_0(k - m\omega^2)}{(k - m\omega^2)^2 + (c\omega)^2}$$

### Step 7: Compute Amplitude

$$U = \sqrt{A^2 + B^2} = \frac{F_0}{\sqrt{(k - m\omega^2)^2 + (c\omega)^2}}$$

Dividing numerator and denominator by $k$:

$$U = \frac{F_0/k}{\sqrt{(1 - \omega^2 m/k)^2 + (c\omega/k)^2}} = \frac{u_{st}}{\sqrt{(1 - r^2)^2 + (2\xi r)^2}}$$

### Step 8: Compute Phase Angle

$$\tan\phi = -\frac{B}{A} = \frac{c\omega}{k - m\omega^2} = \frac{2\xi r}{1 - r^2}$$

$$\phi = \tan^{-1}\left(\frac{2\xi r}{1 - r^2}\right)$$

### Final Result:

$$\boxed{u_{ss}(t) = \frac{u_{st}}{\sqrt{(1 - r^2)^2 + (2\xi r)^2}} \sin(\omega t - \phi)}$$

---

## 3.2 Derivation of Force Transmissibility

### Setup:

When the SDOF system is subjected to a harmonic force, the support reaction (transmitted force) is:

$$F_T(t) = ku(t) + c\dot{u}(t)$$

This is because the spring and damper both connect the mass to the (fixed) support, and they both exert force on the support.

### Substitute Steady-State Response:

$$u_{ss}(t) = U\sin(\omega t - \phi)$$

$$\dot{u}_{ss}(t) = U\omega \cos(\omega t - \phi)$$

Therefore:

$$F_T(t) = kU\sin(\omega t - \phi) + c\omega U\cos(\omega t - \phi)$$

### Express as Single Sinusoid:

The amplitude of $F_T$ is:

$$F_T = \sqrt{(kU)^2 + (c\omega U)^2} = kU\sqrt{1 + (c\omega/k)^2}$$

### Divide by Applied Force:

$$\frac{F_T}{F_0} = \frac{kU\sqrt{1 + (c\omega/k)^2}}{F_0}$$

Substituting $U = F_0/\sqrt{(k - m\omega^2)^2 + (c\omega)^2}$:

$$\frac{F_T}{F_0} = \frac{k\sqrt{1 + (c\omega/k)^2}}{\sqrt{(k - m\omega^2)^2 + (c\omega)^2}}$$

### Normalize with Dimensionless Parameters:

After dividing numerator and denominator by $k$ and using $\xi$, $r$:

$$TR = \sqrt{\frac{1 + (2\xi r)^2}{(1 - r^2)^2 + (2\xi r)^2}}$$

$$\boxed{TR = \sqrt{\frac{1 + (2\xi r)^2}{(1 - r^2)^2 + (2\xi r)^2}}}$$

---

## 3.3 Derivation of Response to Half-Sine Pulse

### Pulse Definition:

$$F(t) = \begin{cases} F_0 \sin(\pi t/t_d), & 0 \le t \le t_d \\ 0, & t > t_d \end{cases}$$

### Phase 1: Forced Vibration During Pulse ($0 \le t \le t_d$)

The equation of motion is:

$$m\ddot{u} + c\dot{u} + ku = F_0 \sin(\pi t/t_d)$$

We treat this as a harmonic load with frequency $\omega = \pi/t_d$. The solution is the sum of transient and steady-state parts:

$$u(t) = e^{-\xi\omega_n t}\left(A\cos\omega_d t + B\sin\omega_d t\right) + U\sin\left(\frac{\pi t}{t_d} - \phi\right)$$

where:

$$U = \frac{F_0/k}{\sqrt{(1 - (\pi/(\omega_n t_d))^2)^2 + (2\xi\pi/(\omega_n t_d))^2}}$$

The constants $A$ and $B$ are determined by initial conditions $u(0) = 0$, $\dot{u}(0) = 0$:

$$A = U\sin\phi$$

$$B = \frac{1}{\omega_d}\left[U\frac{\pi}{t_d}\cos\phi - \xi\omega_n U\sin\phi + \omega_n\xi \cdot 0 - ... \right]$$

(Full expansion is lengthy; key result is the response during the pulse.)

### Phase 2: Free Vibration After Pulse ($t > t_d$)

At $t = t_d$, the pulse ends. The displacement and velocity at $t = t_d$ are computed from Phase 1 and used as initial conditions for free vibration:

$$u(t) = e^{-\xi\omega_n (t - t_d)}\left[u(t_d)\cos\omega_d(t - t_d) + \frac{\dot{u}(t_d) + \xi\omega_n u(t_d)}{\omega_d}\sin\omega_d(t - t_d)\right]$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The response reaches its **maximum value** either during the pulse (forced phase) or just after the pulse ends (free vibration phase). The **response spectrum** of the half-sine pulse plots the maximum response versus $t_d/T_n$ ratio.

---

## 3.4 Derivation of Response to Rectangular Pulse

### Pulse Definition:

$$F(t) = \begin{cases} F_0, & 0 \le t \le t_d \\ 0, & t > t_d \end{cases}$$

This can be viewed as a step-up at $t=0$ followed by a step-down at $t = t_d$.

### Phase 1: Forced Vibration ($0 \le t \le t_d$)

The equation of motion is:

$$m\ddot{u} + c\dot{u} + ku = F_0$$

The solution is:

$$u(t) = e^{-\xi\omega_n t}\left(A\cos\omega_d t + B\sin\omega_d t\right) + u_{st}$$

With $u(0) = 0$, $\dot{u}(0) = 0$:

$$A = -u_{st}$$

$$B = \frac{\xi\omega_n u_{st}}{\omega_d} = \frac{\xi u_{st}}{\sqrt{1 - \xi^2}}$$

So:

$$u(t) = u_{st}\left[1 - e^{-\xi\omega_n t}\left(\cos\omega_d t + \frac{\xi}{\sqrt{1 - \xi^2}}\sin\omega_d t\right)\right]$$

### Phase 2: Free Vibration ($t > t_d$)

At $t = t_d$:

$$u(t_d) = u_{st}\left[1 - e^{-\xi\omega_n t_d}\left(\cos\omega_d t_d + \frac{\xi}{\sqrt{1 - \xi^2}}\sin\omega_d t_d\right)\right]$$

$$\dot{u}(t_d) = \text{computed from derivative}$$

These are used as initial conditions for free vibration.

---

## 3.5 Derivation of Response to Triangular Pulse

### Pulse Definition:

$$F(t) = \begin{cases} F_0(1 - t/t_d), & 0 \le t \le t_d \\ 0, & t > t_d \end{cases}$$

### Analytical Approach:

This is a linearly decreasing load from $F_0$ at $t=0$ to $0$ at $t=t_d$. The equation of motion is:

$$m\ddot{u} + c\dot{u} + ku = F_0(1 - t/t_d), \quad 0 \le t \le t_d$$

The total solution is the sum of:
- Response to a step $F_0$ (constant) 
- Response to a ramp $-F_0 t/t_d$ (linearly decreasing)

Using Duhamel's integral (convolution integral) for the general case:

$$u(t) = \frac{1}{m\omega_d}\int_0^t F(\tau) e^{-\xi\omega_n (t - \tau)}\sin\omega_d(t - \tau)\,d\tau$$

For a triangular pulse, the integral becomes a piecewise expression that can be evaluated in closed form.

> [!NOTE]
> The triangular pulse response spectrum peaks at a slightly different $t_d/T_n$ ratio compared to rectangular and half-sine pulses. Designers consult **response spectra** of all three pulse types to bound the maximum response.

---

## 3.6 Total Response Decomposition — Worked Analytical Form

For a damped SDOF system with initial conditions $u(0) = u_0$, $\dot{u}(0) = v_0$ under arbitrary forcing $F(t)$:

$$\boxed{u(t) = \underbrace{e^{-\xi\omega_n t}\left[u_0 \cos\omega_d t + \frac{v_0 + \xi\omega_n u_0}{\omega_d}\sin\omega_d t\right]}_{u_{tr}(t): \text{Transient}} + \underbrace{\frac{1}{m\omega_d}\int_0^t F(\tau) e^{-\xi\omega_n (t - \tau)}\sin\omega_d(t - \tau)\,d\tau}_{u_{fr}(t): \text{Forced Response (Duhamel)}}}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This is the **Duhamel Integral** (also called the convolution integral). It is the **general solution** for any arbitrary forcing function. The integrand contains the **unit impulse response function** $h(t) = (1/m\omega_d)e^{-\xi\omega_n t}\sin\omega_d t$ multiplied by the forcing function.

The **steady-state amplitude** is recovered by taking $t \to \infty$ in the Duhamel integral for periodic/harmonic loads.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 SDOF System Under Harmonic Force — Physical Setup

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Fixed Support / Ground] -->|Spring k| B[Mass m]
    A -->|Damper c| B
    P[Harmonic Force F₀sin ωt] -->|Applied| B
    B -->|Transmitted Force F_T| A
    style A fill:#1f3a5f,color:#ffffff
    style B fill:#c84b31,color:#ffffff
    style P fill:#2d6a4f,color:#ffffff
```

**Description:** The diagram shows a mass-spring-damper SDOF system fixed to a rigid base. A harmonic force $F_0 \sin\omega t$ is applied to the mass. The transmitted force $F_T$ goes back into the support through the spring and damper.

---

## 4.2 Response Decomposition — Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[Input: Force F of t] --> B[Forced Vibration Solver]
    A --> C[Initial Conditions]
    C --> D[Free Vibration Solver]
    B --> E[Steady State Component u_ss]
    D --> F[Transient Component u_tr]
    E --> G[Superposition]
    F --> G
    G --> H[Total Response u of t]
    style A fill:#264653,color:#ffffff
    style B fill:#2a9d8f,color:#ffffff
    style C fill:#e9c46a,color:#000000
    style D fill:#f4a261,color:#000000
    style E fill:#e76f51,color:#ffffff
    style F fill:#e76f51,color:#ffffff
    style G fill:#6a4c93,color:#ffffff
    style H fill:#1982c4,color:#ffffff
```

**Description:** This block diagram shows how total response is built: the forcing function drives a forced-vibration solver producing steady-state response, while initial conditions drive a free-vibration solver producing transient response. Both are superposed to yield total response.

---

## 4.3 Frequency Response & Transmissibility Curves

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph LowFreq["LOW FREQUENCY: r LESS THAN 1"]
        LF1[Static-like response, D near 1]
        LF2[Phase lag near 0 degrees]
    end
    subgraph Res["RESONANCE: r EQUALS 1"]
        R1[Maximum amplification for small damping]
        R2[Phase lag equals 90 degrees]
    end
    subgraph HighFreq["HIGH FREQUENCY: r GREATER THAN sqrt 2"]
        HF1[D less than 1: response smaller than static]
        HF2[TR less than 1: force isolation achieved]
        HF3[Phase lag near 180 degrees]
    end
    LowFreq --> Res
    Res --> HighFreq
    style LowFreq fill:#a8dadc,color:#000000
    style Res fill:#f1faee,color:#000000
    style HighFreq fill:#457b9d,color:#ffffff
```

**Description:** The response of an SDOF system changes character dramatically with the frequency ratio $r$. The diagram highlights the three key regimes.

---

## 4.4 Pulse Shape Comparison

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[Impulsive Load Classification] --> B[Ideal Impulse: Dirac Delta]
    A --> C[Finite Duration Pulses]
    C --> D[Rectangular: Constant F0 over td]
    C --> E[Half-sine: Sinusoidal arc]
    C --> F[Triangular: Linear rise and fall]
    A --> G[Key Parameter: td / Tn ratio]
    G --> H[If td much less than Tn: Equivalent to ideal impulse]
    G --> I[If td comparable to Tn: Full pulse shape matters]
    G --> J[If td much greater than Tn: Quasi-static loading]
    style A fill:#1d3557,color:#ffffff
    style B fill:#e63946,color:#ffffff
    style C fill:#457b9d,color:#ffffff
    style D fill:#f1faee,color:#000000
    style E fill:#f1faee,color:#000000
    style F fill:#f1faee,color:#000000
    style G fill:#2a9d8f,color:#ffffff
    style H fill:#a8dadc,color:#000000
    style I fill:#a8dadc,color:#000000
    style J fill:#a8dadc,color:#000000
```

**Description:** Impulsive loads can be idealized as a Dirac delta or modeled with finite-duration shapes (rectangular, half-sine, triangular). The critical parameter is the ratio of pulse duration $t_d$ to natural period $T_n$.

---

## 4.5 Vibration Isolation Strategy

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Source Machine] -->|Vibratory Force| B[Isolator: Spring + Damper]
    B -->|Transmitted Force| C[Sensitive Structure]
    A --> D{Choose omega_n}
    D -->|omega_n much less than omega| E[Isolation Achieved]
    D -->|omega_n near omega| F[Resonance: Catastrophic Amplification]
    D -->|omega_n much greater than omega| G[No Isolation, Force Transmitted]
    style A fill:#264653,color:#ffffff
    style B fill:#2a9d8f,color:#ffffff
    style C fill:#e76f51,color:#ffffff
    style D fill:#f4a261,color:#000000
    style E fill:#06d6a0,color:#000000
    style F fill:#ef476f,color:#ffffff
    style G fill:#ffd166,color:#000000
```

**Description:** This diagram illustrates the design philosophy of vibration isolation. To achieve isolation, the natural frequency $\omega_n$ of the isolator should be made much smaller than the forcing frequency $\omega$ of the disturbing machine.

---

## 4.6 Fourier Series Decomposition of Periodic Load

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Periodic Load F of t with Period Tp] --> B[Compute Fourier Coefficients]
    B --> B1[a0: Mean Value]
    B --> B2[a1 cos omega p t: 1st Harmonic]
    B --> B3[a2 cos 2 omega p t: 2nd Harmonic]
    B --> B4[an cos n omega p t: nth Harmonic]
    B --> C[Solve SDOF Response to Each]
    C --> D[Sum All Harmonic Responses]
    D --> E[Total Periodic Response]
    style A fill:#1d3557,color:#ffffff
    style B fill:#457b9d,color:#ffffff
    style B1 fill:#a8dadc,color:#000000
    style B2 fill:#a8dadc,color:#000000
    style B3 fill:#a8dadc,color:#000000
    style B4 fill:#a8dadc,color:#000000
    style C fill:#f1faee,color:#000000
    style D fill:#2a9d8f,color:#ffffff
    style E fill:#e63946,color:#ffffff
```

**Description:** This diagram shows the workflow for analyzing SDOF response to periodic loading: decompose the load using Fourier series, solve the response for each harmonic, then superpose.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]
**Define Dynamic Magnification Factor. What is its value at resonance for a system with damping ratio $\xi$?**

**Model Answer:**

The Dynamic Magnification Factor (DMF or $D$) is defined as the ratio of the steady-state dynamic amplitude to the static deflection produced by the same force:

$$D = \frac{U}{u_{st}} = \frac{1}{\sqrt{(1 - r^2)^2 + (2\xi r)^2}}$$

At resonance, the frequency ratio $r = 1$. Substituting:

$$D_{res} = \frac{1}{\sqrt{(1 - 1)^2 + (2\xi)^2}} = \frac{1}{2\xi}$$

**For an undamped system** ($\xi = 0$): $D \to \infty$ (resonance catastrophe).
**For a damped system**: $D = 1/(2\xi)$ (finite peak).

---

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam – June 2023]
**What is meant by force transmissibility? State the condition for effective vibration isolation.**

**Model Answer:**

**Force transmissibility (TR)** is defined as the ratio of the amplitude of the force transmitted to the support to the amplitude of the applied harmonic force:

$$TR = \frac{F_T}{F_0} = \sqrt{\frac{1 + (2\xi r)^2}{(1 - r^2)^2 + (2\xi r)^2}}$$

**Condition for effective vibration isolation:** The frequency ratio must satisfy $r > \sqrt{2}$ (i.e., $\omega > \sqrt{2}\,\omega_n$). In this region, $TR < 1$ for **any value of damping**, meaning the force transmitted to the support is less than the applied force. Greater isolation is achieved with lower damping in this region.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### QUESTION A [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]

**(a)** An SDOF system with mass $m = 50$ kg, stiffness $k = 20{,}000$ N/m, and damping ratio $\xi = 0.05$ is subjected to a harmonic force $F(t) = 1000 \sin(20t)$ N. Determine:
1. The natural frequency $\omega_n$
2. The frequency ratio $r$
3. The steady-state amplitude $U$
4. The phase angle $\phi$

**(b)** For the same system, calculate the **force transmissibility** and comment on whether the system acts as a good vibration isolator at this forcing frequency.

#### MODEL SOLUTION (a)

**Step 1: Natural Frequency**

$$\omega_n = \sqrt{\frac{k}{m}} = \sqrt{\frac{20{,}000}{50}} = \sqrt{400} = 20 \text{ rad/s}$$

**Step 2: Frequency Ratio**

$$r = \frac{\omega}{\omega_n} = \frac{20}{20} = 1.0$$

This is **resonance**.

**Step 3: Static Deflection**

$$u_{st} = \frac{F_0}{k} = \frac{1000}{20{,}000} = 0.05 \text{ m}$$

**Step 4: Steady-State Amplitude**

$$U = \frac{u_{st}}{\sqrt{(1 - r^2)^2 + (2\xi r)^2}} = \frac{0.05}{\sqrt{(1 - 1)^2 + (2 \times 0.05 \times 1)^2}}$$

$$U = \frac{0.05}{\sqrt{0 + 0.01}} = \frac{0.05}{0.1} = 0.5 \text{ m}$$

**Step 5: Phase Angle**

$$\phi = \tan^{-1}\left(\frac{2\xi r}{1 - r^2}\right) = \tan^{-1}\left(\frac{0.1}{0}\right) = 90°$$

#### MODEL SOLUTION (b)

**Step 1: Transmissibility**

$$TR = \sqrt{\frac{1 + (2\xi r)^2}{(1 - r^2)^2 + (2\xi r)^2}} = \sqrt{\frac{1 + 0.01}{0 + 0.01}} = \sqrt{\frac{1.01}{0.01}} = \sqrt{101} \approx 10.05$$

**Step 2: Comment on Isolation**

Since $r = 1$ (resonance), $TR \gg 1$ (about 10×). The system **amplifies the transmitted force** by 10 times. 

- $r = 1$ is **NOT in the isolation region** ($r > \sqrt{2}$ required).
- The system is a **very poor isolator** at this frequency.
- In fact, it is in the most dangerous region — operating at resonance will cause large oscillations and high transmitted forces that can damage the support structure.

**Recommendation:** Modify the system stiffness so that $r \neq 1$. If isolation is desired, lower $\omega_n$ to push $r > \sqrt{2}$. If resonance must be avoided, shift $\omega_n$ away from $\omega$ by at least 20–30%.

---

### QUESTION B [KTU University Exam – June 2022]

**(a)** A machine of mass 200 kg operates at 1500 rpm and produces a harmonic unbalanced force of amplitude 2000 N. Design an isolator (i.e., find the stiffness $k$) such that the transmissibility is 0.10. Assume damping ratio $\xi = 0$ (undamped isolator). Also, calculate the steady-state amplitude of the mass.

**(b)** Plot a sketch of the force transmissibility curve $TR$ vs $r$ for $\xi = 0$, clearly marking the isolation region and the resonance peak.

#### MODEL SOLUTION (a)

**Step 1: Forcing Frequency**

$$\omega = \frac{2\pi N}{60} = \frac{2\pi \times 1500}{60} = 50\pi \approx 157.08 \text{ rad/s}$$

**Step 2: Apply Transmissibility Formula for $\xi = 0$**

$$TR = \frac{1}{\vert 1 - r^2 \vert} = 0.10$$

For isolation, $r > 1$, so $(1 - r^2)$ is negative, but $TR$ uses the absolute value:

$$1 - r^2 = \frac{1}{0.10} \cdot (-1) = -10$$

$$r^2 = 11, \quad r = \sqrt{11} \approx 3.317$$

**Step 3: Solve for Stiffness**

$$r = \frac{\omega}{\omega_n} = \omega \sqrt{\frac{m}{k}}$$

$$\sqrt{\frac{m}{k}} = \frac{r}{\omega}$$

$$k = m \left(\frac{\omega}{r}\right)^2 = 200 \times \left(\frac{157.08}{3.317}\right)^2$$

$$k = 200 \times (47.36)^2 = 200 \times 2242.97 \approx 4.486 \times 10^5 \text{ N/m}$$

$$\boxed{k \approx 448.6 \text{ kN/m}}$$

**Step 4: Steady-State Amplitude**

$$U = \frac{u_{st}}{\vert 1 - r^2 \vert} = \frac{F_0/k}{\vert 1 - r^2 \vert} = \frac{2000/448600}{10} = \frac{0.00446}{10} = 4.46 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}$$

$$\boxed{U \approx 0.446 \text{ mm}}$$

#### MODEL SOLUTION (b)

**Sketch of TR vs r (for $\xi = 0$):**

- **For $r < 1$:** $TR = 1/(1 - r^2) > 1$, increasing from 1 to $\infty$ as $r \to 1$.
- **At $r = 1$:** $TR = \infty$ (resonance spike).
- **For $r > 1$:** $TR = 1/(r^2 - 1)$, decreasing from $\infty$ to 0 as $r \to \infty$.
- **At $r = \sqrt{2}$:** $TR = 1$ (the cross-over point).
- **For $r > \sqrt{2}$:** $TR < 1$ → **isolation region** (shaded).
- The curve is **asymptotic** to the $r$-axis as $r \to \infty$.

The isolation region begins at $r = \sqrt{2}$ and continues for all $r > \sqrt{2}$. Beyond this point, increasing $r$ (i.e., making the system softer) reduces transmissibility.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes in KTU Board Examinations:**
> 
> 1. **Confusing $u_{st}$ with $U$**: $u_{st}$ is the static deflection ($F_0/k$), and $U$ is the dynamic amplitude. Many students write $U = u_{st}$ without applying the magnification factor. Always compute $D$ separately.
> 
> 2. **Forgetting the absolute value in $TR$ formula**: For $r < 1$, $(1 - r^2)$ is positive, but the formula still works because of the squaring. However, when isolating variables, students forget to use $r^2 - 1$ for $r > 1$ in undamped cases.
> 
> 3. **Sign error in phase angle**: The phase angle $\phi$ is measured as a **lag** of response behind the force. For $r > 1$, the formula gives $\phi > 90°$. Don't write it as a negative number.
> 
> 4. **Mixing up transient and steady-state solutions**: When asked for the total response, students sometimes write only the steady-state form, missing the transient. The full solution is $u(t) = u_{tr}(t) + u_{ss}(t)$.
> 
> 5. **Forgetting to convert rpm to rad/s**: A common error is to use $N$ (in rpm) directly as $\omega$. Always convert: $\omega = 2\pi N/60$.
> 
> 6. **Incorrect Fourier coefficient integrals**: For symmetric loads, the sine coefficients $b_n$ vanish (even function). Students sometimes compute unnecessary terms.
> 
> 7. **Not stating units in final answers**: KTU examiners deduct marks for missing units in numerical answers.
> 
> 8. **Pulse-shape response confusion**: Each pulse shape (rectangular, half-sine, triangular) has a different response spectrum. Don't memorize a single formula and apply it to all pulse types.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

### Rapid Revision Checklist

- [x] **Harmonic force form**: $F(t) = F_0\sin(\omega t)$ or $F_0\cos(\omega t)$
- [x] **Steady-state amplitude**: $U = u_{st}/\sqrt{(1 - r^2)^2 + (2\xi r)^2}$
- [x] **DMF**: $D = 1/\sqrt{(1 - r^2)^2 + (2\xi r)^2}$, equals $1/(2\xi)$ at resonance
- [x] **Phase angle**: $\phi = \tan^{-1}(2\xi r/(1 - r^2))$
- [x] **Total response**: $u(t) = u_{tr}(t) + u_{ss}(t)$
- [x] **Transient dies out** in $\approx 4/(\xi\omega_n)$ seconds
- [x] **Force transmissibility**: $TR = \sqrt{(1 + (2\xi r)^2)/[(1 - r^2)^2 + (2\xi r)^2]}$
- [x] **Isolation condition**: $r > \sqrt{2}$ for **any** $\xi$
- [x] **Cross-over points**: $D = 1$ and $TR = 1$ both occur at $r = \sqrt{2}$ (for $\xi \ne 0$)
- [x] **Fourier series**: $F(t) = a_0 + \sum(a_n\cos n\omega_p t + b_n\sin n\omega_p t)$, $\omega_p = 2\pi/T_p$
- [x] **Symmetry simplifications**: Even function → $b_n = 0$; Odd function → $a_n = 0$
- [x] **Superposition**: Response to periodic load = sum of responses to each Fourier component
- [x] **Impulse response**: $u(t) = (\hat{F}/m\omega_d)e^{-\xi\omega_n t}\sin(\omega_d t)$ (ideal impulse)
- [x] **Three pulse shapes**: Rectangular, half-sine, triangular — each with its own response spectrum
- [x] **Key parameter for pulses**: $t_d/T_n$ ratio determines pulse behavior
- [x] **Duhamel integral** is the general solution for arbitrary forcing

### Key Numerical Conversions

- $1$ Hz $= 2\pi$ rad/s
- $\omega$ (rad/s) $= 2\pi N/60$ where $N$ is in rpm
- $f$ (Hz) $= N/60$
- $T = 2\pi/\omega_n = 1/f_n$

### Critical Formulas at a Glance

| Symbol | Meaning | Magnitude at Resonance |
|--------|---------|------------------------|
| $D$ | Magnification factor | $1/(2\xi)$ |
| $\phi$ | Phase angle | $90°$ |
| $TR$ (undamped) | Transmissibility | $\infty$ |
| $TR$ (damped) | Transmissibility | $\sqrt{(1 + 4\xi^2)/(4\xi^2)}$ |

### Quick Mental Tests

1. **Is the system isolated?** → Check $r > \sqrt{2}$
2. **Is the system in resonance?** → Check $r \approx 1$
3. **Is the damping important?** → Only near resonance ($r \approx 0.7$ to $1.5$); negligible far from resonance
4. **Is the pulse "fast" or "slow"?** → Compare $t_d$ to $T_n$

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Natural Frequencies and Mode Shapes, Orthogonality of Normal Modes
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECET741 - STRUCTURAL DYNAMICS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Natural Frequencies and Mode Shapes, Orthogonality of Normal Modes

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!NOTE]
> **Natural Frequency ($\omega_n$):** The frequency at which a structural system naturally tends to vibrate when displaced from its static equilibrium position and released, in the absence of any external forcing or damping. For an **N-degree-of-freedom (N-DOF)** system, there exist exactly **N** natural frequencies ($\omega_1, \omega_2, \ldots, \omega_N$), conventionally arranged in ascending order: $\omega_1 < \omega_2 < \ldots < \omega_N$.

> [!NOTE]
> **Mode Shape (Normal Mode) ($\{\phi\}$):** The corresponding deflected configuration (a vector) that a system assumes while vibrating at one of its natural frequencies. Each natural frequency has a unique mode shape associated with it, representing the **relative amplitude and phase** of motion of every mass coordinate.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Syllabus Highlight:** The collection of all N natural frequencies and N mode shapes is collectively called the **modal properties** of the structure. They depend **only** on the mass distribution $[M]$ and stiffness distribution $[K]$ — *not* on the applied load.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Building & The Guitar String

Imagine a tall, slender building swaying during an earthquake. It does not merely translate as a rigid block; instead, it deforms in characteristic patterns.

- **First Mode (Fundamental Mode):** The whole structure sways back and forth like an inverted pendulum — simplest "S-shape." This corresponds to the **lowest** natural frequency $\omega_1$.
- **Second Mode:** The building forms one full wave — the base moves one way while the top moves the other. Higher frequency $\omega_2$.
- **Third Mode:** Two inflection points — even higher frequency $\omega_3$.

A guitar string gives an identical intuition: plucking it lightly excites the **first mode** (one hump), while plucking sharply at the center excites the **second mode** (two humps). The string "prefers" only those exact shapes, dictated entirely by its tension (stiffness) and mass per unit length.

> [!TIP]
> **Engineering Rule of Thumb:** Always remember that **mode shapes are dimensionless ratios** — only their *shape* is unique, not their absolute magnitude. Hence, we typically **normalize** them (e.g., set the largest entry to 1).

## 1.3 The Eigenvalue Problem in Plain English

The complete set $\{(\omega_i, \{\phi_i\})\}_{i=1}^{N}$ is obtained by solving a mathematical construct known as the **generalized eigenvalue problem**:

$$[K]\{\phi_i\} = \omega_i^2 [M]\{\phi_i\}$$

Physically: the **restoring (stiffness) force vector** equals the **mass-times-acceleration** vector, evaluated *at the natural mode of vibration*. The "natural" values of $\omega^2$ (eigenvalues) for which a non-zero $\{\phi\}$ (eigenvector) exists are precisely the natural frequencies squared.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Logic — Step-by-Step Theoretical Flow

Consider an undamped free-vibration MDOF system governed by:

$$[M]\{\ddot{x}(t)\} + [K]\{x(t)\} = \{0\}$$

where $[M], [K] \in \mathbb{R}^{N \times N}$ are symmetric, positive-definite matrices.

**Step 1 — Assume Harmonic Motion.** Because the system is linear with constant coefficients, the solution must be a superposition of simple harmonic motions. We postulate:

$$\{x(t)\} = \{\phi\} \sin(\omega t + \theta)$$

where $\{\phi\}$ is a constant vector describing the spatial pattern (mode shape) and $\omega$ is the trial frequency.

**Step 2 — Substitute into the EOM.** Differentiating twice with respect to time:

$$\{\ddot{x}(t)\} = -\omega^2 \{\phi\} \sin(\omega t + \theta)$$

Substituting both into the EOM and factoring out $\sin(\omega t + \theta)$:

$$\left( [K] - \omega^2 [M] \right) \{\phi\} = \{0\}$$

**Step 3 — Enforce the Non-Triviality Condition.** The trivial solution $\{\phi\} = \{0\}$ represents the system sitting still (no motion), which is mathematically valid for *any* $\omega$ but physically uninteresting. To obtain a vibration, we **must** have:

$$\det\left( [K] - \omega^2 [M] \right) = 0$$

This is the **frequency equation** (or characteristic equation). Expanding the determinant yields a polynomial of degree $N$ in $\lambda = \omega^2$:

$$\lambda^N + a_1 \lambda^{N-1} + a_2 \lambda^{N-2} + \cdots + a_{N-1} \lambda + a_N = 0$$

**Step 4 — Solve for Eigenvalues.** The N roots $\lambda_i = \omega_i^2$ (with $\omega_i > 0$ since $[K]$ and $[M]$ are positive-definite) are the **squared natural frequencies**.

**Step 5 — Back-Substitute for Eigenvectors.** For each $\omega_i^2$, substitute back into the rank-deficient system to solve for $\{\phi_i\}$ up to an arbitrary scale factor.

## 2.2 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Relation | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\left( [K] - \omega^2 [M] \right)\{\phi\} = \{0\}$ | Generalized eigenvalue equation | — |
| 2 | $\det\left( [K] - \omega^2 [M] \right) = 0$ | Frequency (characteristic) equation | — |
| 3 | $\lambda_i = \omega_i^2$ | Eigenvalue relation | $(\text{rad/s})^2$ |
| 4 | $\{\phi_i\}^T [M] \{\phi_j\} = 0,\ i \ne j$ | Mass orthogonality | kg or kg·m² |
| 5 | $\{\phi_i\}^T [K] \{\phi_j\} = 0,\ i \ne j$ | Stiffness orthogonality | N/m or N |
| 6 | $\{\phi_i\}^T [M] \{\phi_i\} = M_i$ (modal mass) | Diagonal of $[M]$ in modal coords. | kg |
| 7 | $\{\phi_i\}^T [K] \{\phi_i\} = K_i = \omega_i^2 M_i$ (modal stiffness) | Diagonal of $[K]$ in modal coords. | N/m |
| 8 | $\{\psi_i\} = \dfrac{\{\phi_i\}}{\sqrt{M_i}}$ | Mass-normalized mode shape | $1/\sqrt{\text{kg}}$ |
| 9 | $\{\psi_i\}^T [M] \{\psi_j\} = \delta_{ij}$ (Kronecker delta) | Ortho-normal mass relation | — |
| 10 | $\{\psi_i\}^T [K] \{\psi_j\} = \omega_i^2 \delta_{ij}$ | Ortho-normal stiffness relation | — |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Pitfall Note:** Orthogonality is strictly a property of *distinct* modes. If two modes share the same natural frequency (a *repeated* eigenvalue), the proof breaks down and we must construct orthogonal vectors via **modal assurance criterion (MAC)** or Gram–Schmidt. For KTU 2024 exams, the standard assumption is **distinct natural frequencies**.

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Earthquake Engineering:** Modal superposition allows the seismic response of a 1000-DOF building to be computed as a sum of 1000 independent single-DOF problems — a massive computational reduction.
- **Aerospace:** Flutter analysis of aircraft wings relies on identifying modes that couple aerodynamic and structural frequencies.
- **Rotating Machinery:** Misalignment and unbalance forces are diagnosed by comparing forcing frequencies to the structure's natural frequencies (resonance).
- **Civil Structures:** Avoiding resonance with walking frequencies (~2 Hz) in footbridges; avoiding resonance with wind vortex shedding in chimneys (Hartlen–Currie criterion).

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Generalized Eigenvalue Problem

Starting from the **free-vibration equation of motion** for an undamped MDOF system:

$$[M]\{\ddot{x}(t)\} + [K]\{x(t)\} = \{0\} \quad \text{(Eq. 3.1)}$$

**Step A.** Assume a synchronous (harmonic) motion ansatz:

$$\{x(t)\} = \{\phi\} \, e^{i\omega t}$$

where $\{\phi\}$ is a complex vector of mode amplitudes and $i = \sqrt{-1}$.

**Step B.** Differentiate twice with respect to time:

$$\{\ddot{x}(t)\} = -\omega^2 \{\phi\} \, e^{i\omega t}$$

**Step C.** Substitute into Eq. (3.1):

$$[M](-\omega^2 \{\phi\} \, e^{i\omega t}) + [K](\{\phi\} \, e^{i\omega t}) = \{0\}$$

**Step D.** Factor out the common scalar $e^{i\omega t}$ (which is never zero):

$$\left( [K] - \omega^2 [M] \right) \{\phi\} = \{0\} \quad \text{(Eq. 3.2)}$$

**Step E.** The system in Eq. (3.2) is a **homogeneous linear system** in $\{\phi\}$. For it to admit a non-trivial solution ($\{\phi\} \ne \{0\}$), the determinant of the coefficient matrix must vanish:

$$\det\left( [K] - \omega^2 [M] \right) = 0 \quad \text{(Eq. 3.3)}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Mathematical Interpretation:** Eq. (3.2) defines a *generalized eigenvalue problem*. The values of $\omega^2$ that satisfy Eq. (3.3) are the eigenvalues, and the corresponding $\{\phi\}$ are the eigenvectors.

## 3.2 Derivation of the Orthogonality of Normal Modes

**Statement.** For two distinct modes $i$ and $j$ of an undamped MDOF system with $\omega_i \ne \omega_j$, the mode shapes satisfy the orthogonality relations:

$$\{\phi_i\}^T [M] \{\phi_j\} = 0 \quad \text{and} \quad \{\phi_i\}^T [K] \{\phi_j\} = 0 \quad \text{for } i \ne j$$

**Proof.**

**Step 1.** By definition of the eigenvalue problem applied to mode $i$:

$$[K] \{\phi_i\} = \omega_i^2 [M] \{\phi_i\} \quad \text{(Eq. 3.4)}$$

**Step 2.** Pre-multiply both sides of Eq. (3.4) by $\{\phi_j\}^T$:

$$\{\phi_j\}^T [K] \{\phi_i\} = \omega_i^2 \{\phi_j\}^T [M] \{\phi_i\} \quad \text{(Eq. 3.5)}$$

**Step 3.** Apply the analogous eigenvalue relation for mode $j$:

$$[K] \{\phi_j\} = \omega_j^2 [M] \{\phi_j\} \quad \text{(Eq. 3.6)}$$

**Step 4.** Pre-multiply Eq. (3.6) by $\{\phi_i\}^T$:

$$\{\phi_i\}^T [K] \{\phi_j\} = \omega_j^2 \{\phi_i\}^T [M] \{\phi_j\} \quad \text{(Eq. 3.7)}$$

**Step 5.** Since $[K]$ and $[M]$ are real symmetric matrices, $[K]^T = [K]$ and $[M]^T = [M]$. Therefore the left-hand side of Eq. (3.5) can be rewritten using the identity $(AB)^T = B^T A^T$:

$$\{\phi_j\}^T [K] \{\phi_i\} = \left( \{\phi_j\}^T [K] \{\phi_i\} \right)^T = \{\phi_i\}^T [K]^T \{\phi_j\} = \{\phi_i\}^T [K] \{\phi_j\}$$

Hence Eq. (3.5) and Eq. (3.7) share the same left-hand side. Equating the right-hand sides:

$$\omega_i^2 \{\phi_j\}^T [M] \{\phi_i\} = \omega_j^2 \{\phi_i\}^T [M] \{\phi_j\}$$

**Step 6.** Using the same symmetry argument, $\{\phi_j\}^T [M] \{\phi_i\} = \{\phi_i\}^T [M] \{\phi_j\}$. Substituting:

$$\omega_i^2 \{\phi_i\}^T [M] \{\phi_j\} = \omega_j^2 \{\phi_i\}^T [M] \{\phi_j\}$$

**Step 7.** Rearranging:

$$\left( \omega_i^2 - \omega_j^2 \right) \{\phi_i\}^T [M] \{\phi_j\} = 0 \quad \text{(Eq. 3.8)}$$

**Step 8.** Since we assume **distinct natural frequencies** ($\omega_i \ne \omega_j$, so $\omega_i^2 \ne \omega_j^2$), the scalar multiplier is non-zero. By the **zero-product property of real numbers**, the second factor must vanish:

$$\{\phi_i\}^T [M] \{\phi_j\} = 0 \quad \text{for } i \ne j \quad \text{(Mass orthogonality)} \quad \blacksquare$$

**Step 9.** Substituting this result back into Eq. (3.7) immediately yields stiffness orthogonality:

$$\{\phi_i\}^T [K] \{\phi_j\} = \omega_j^2 \cdot 0 = 0 \quad \text{(Stiffness orthogonality)} \quad \blacksquare$$

## 3.3 Mass-Normalized Mode Shapes

The arbitrarily-scaled mode shape $\{\phi_i\}$ can be uniquely normalized with respect to the mass matrix by defining:

$$\{\psi_i\} = \frac{\{\phi_i\}}{\sqrt{\{\phi_i\}^T [M] \{\phi_i\}}} = \frac{\{\phi_i\}}{\sqrt{M_i}} \quad \text{(Eq. 3.9)}$$

This gives the elegant **ortho-normal relations**:

$$\{\psi_i\}^T [M] \{\psi_j\} = \delta_{ij}, \qquad \{\psi_i\}^T [K] \{\psi_j\} = \omega_i^2 \, \delta_{ij}$$

where $\delta_{ij}$ is the Kronecker delta ($\delta_{ij} = 1$ if $i = j$, else $0$). The modal matrix $\Psi = [\{\psi_1\} \ \ \{\psi_2\} \ \ \cdots \ \ \{\psi_N\}]$ therefore **diagonalizes** both $[M]$ and $[K]$ simultaneously:

$$\Psi^T [M] \Psi = [I], \qquad \Psi^T [K] \Psi = \text{diag}(\omega_1^2, \omega_2^2, \ldots, \omega_N^2)$$

## 3.4 Worked Example — 2-DOF System Verification

Consider a 2-DOF spring-mass system with:
$$[M] = \begin{bmatrix} m & 0 \\ 0 & m \end{bmatrix}, \qquad [K] = \begin{bmatrix} 2k & -k \\ -k & 2k \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 1.** Form the determinant:
$$\det\left( [K] - \omega^2 [M] \right) = \det\begin{bmatrix} 2k - m\omega^2 & -k \\ -k & 2k - m\omega^2 \end{bmatrix} = 0$$

**Step 2.** Expand:
$$(2k - m\omega^2)^2 - k^2 = 0$$

**Step 3.** Take square roots (or solve as a quadratic in $\lambda = m\omega^2/k$):
$$2k - m\omega^2 = \pm k$$

$$m\omega^2 = 2k \mp k$$

**Step 4.** Natural frequencies:
$$\omega_1^2 = \frac{k}{m} \quad \Rightarrow \quad \omega_1 = \sqrt{\frac{k}{m}}$$
$$\omega_2^2 = \frac{3k}{m} \quad \Rightarrow \quad \omega_2 = \sqrt{\frac{3k}{m}}$$

**Step 5.** Mode shape for $\omega_1^2 = k/m$. From row 1 of $[K]\{\phi_1\} = \omega_1^2[M]\{\phi_1\}$:
$$(2k - k)\phi_{1,1} - k \phi_{1,2} = 0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \phi_{1,2} = \phi_{1,1}$$

So $\{\phi_1\} = \begin{Bmatrix} 1 \\ 1 \end{Bmatrix}$ (in-phase, symmetric mode).

**Step 6.** Mode shape for $\omega_2^2 = 3k/m$:
$$(2k - 3k)\phi_{2,1} - k \phi_{2,2} = 0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \phi_{2,2} = -\phi_{2,1}$$

So $\{\phi_2\} = \begin{Bmatrix} 1 \\ -1 \end{Bmatrix}$ (out-of-phase, anti-symmetric mode).

**Step 7.** Verify orthogonality:
$$\{\phi_1\}^T [M] \{\phi_2\} = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 1 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} m & 0 \\ 0 & m \end{bmatrix} \begin{Bmatrix} 1 \\ -1 \end{Bmatrix} = m(1)(1) + m(1)(-1) = 0 \quad \checkmark$$

$$\{\phi_1\}^T [K] \{\phi_2\} = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 1 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} 2k & -k \\ -k & 2k \end{bmatrix} \begin{Bmatrix} 1 \\ -1 \end{Bmatrix} = (2k - k)(1) + (-k + 2k)(-1) = k - k = 0 \quad \checkmark$$

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Architecture — Eigenvalue Problem Solution Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Input System Matrices M and K] --> B[Form Dynamic Matrix D of K minus omega squared M]
    B --> C[Set Determinant of D to Zero]
    C --> D[Solve Characteristic Polynomial for lambda equals omega squared]
    D --> E{N Distinct Real Positive Roots}
    E -- Yes --> F[Sort Frequencies Ascending omega1 lt omega2 lt ... lt omegaN]
    E -- No --> G[Special Case: Repeated Roots or Complex Roots]
    F --> H[Back Substitute Each omega i squared into K minus omega squared M times phi equals zero]
    H --> I[Extract Eigenvector phi i from Rank Deficient System]
    I --> J[Normalize: Set Largest Entry to 1 OR Mass Normalize to psi i]
    J --> K[Verify Orthogonality: phi i transposed M phi j equals 0 and phi i transposed K phi j equals 0]
    K --> L[Output Modal Set: omega i and phi i for i equals 1 to N]
    G --> M[Apply Gram Schmidt or Deflation Techniques]
    M --> F
```

## 4.2 Physical Representation of Mode Shapes for a 2-DOF Cantilever-Type Shear Building

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph MODE1[FIRST MODE: omega1 equals sqrt k over m]
        direction LR
        G1[Ground] --> S1A((m1))
        S1A -- Spring k1 --> S1B((m2))
        S1B --> S1C[Free End]
        M1A[Phi1,1 = +1] -.-> S1A
        M1B[Phi1,2 = +1] -.-> S1B
    end

    subgraph MODE2[SECOND MODE: omega2 equals sqrt 3k over m]
        direction LR
        G2[Ground] --> S2A((m1))
        S2A -- Spring k1 --> S2B((m2))
        S2B --> S2C[Free End]
        M2A[Phi2,1 = +1] -.-> S2A
        M2B[Phi2,2 = -1] -.-> S2B
    end
```

## 4.3 Conceptual Node Map — From Physical System to Modal Response

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P0[Physical N-DOF System: Distributed Mass and Stiffness] --> P1[Discretize: Assemble Lumped M and K Matrices]
    P1 --> P2[Apply Boundary Conditions: Pin Supports, Fixed Ends]
    P2 --> P3[Form Eigenproblem: K phi = omega squared M phi]
    P3 --> P4{Use Numerical Solver}
    P4 --> P4a[For Small N: Direct Determinant Method]
    P4 --> P4b[For Large N: Lanczos, Subspace Iteration, or QR Algorithm]
    P4a --> P5[Modal Set: Frequencies and Mode Shapes]
    P4b --> P5
    P5 --> P6[Engineer Uses Modes For: Modal Superposition, Response Spectrum, Damage Detection]
```

## 4.4 Mode-Shape Visualization Logic (3-DOF Example)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    subgraph M1[MODE 1: All Masses Move In-Phase]
        D1A((+1)) --- D1B((+1)) --- D1C((+1))
    end
    subgraph M2[MODE 2: Outer Masses in Phase, Center Out of Phase]
        D2A((+1)) --- D2B((-1.414)) --- D2C((+1))
    end
    subgraph M3[MODE 3: Alternating Pattern]
        D3A((+1)) --- D3B((-1)) --- D3C((+1))
    end
```

> [!TIP]
> **Reading the Diagrams:** Each circle represents a mass location; the sign inside denotes the *relative* displacement direction. A node (zero-crossing) between masses indicates a stationary point — a hallmark of higher modes.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
> **[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model Question]**
> **Define natural frequency and mode shape for a multi-degree-of-freedom system. Why are these called "modal properties"?**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

**Natural Frequency:** The discrete frequencies at which an undamped, freely-vibrating MDOF system oscillates when displaced from equilibrium and released. For an N-DOF system, there are N natural frequencies $\omega_1 < \omega_2 < \ldots < \omega_N$ (each in rad/s or Hz). **(1 Mark)**

**Mode Shape:** The corresponding deflected configuration assumed by the system when vibrating at a particular natural frequency. It is a vector $\{\phi_i\}$ that represents the relative amplitude and phase of motion of all mass coordinates. **(1 Mark)**

**Why "Modal Properties":** Together, the pair $(\omega_i, \{\phi_i\})$ describes a *mode* of vibration — a unique, inherent characteristic of the system. They are called "properties" because they depend **only** on $[M]$ and $[K]$, not on the applied loading or initial conditions. **(1 Mark)**

---

### Question 2
> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model Question]**
> **State the orthogonality conditions of normal modes with respect to the mass and stiffness matrices. Why is this property so important in structural dynamics?**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

**Statement (1.5 Marks):** For two distinct modes $i$ and $j$ of an undamped MDOF system:

$$\{\phi_i\}^T [M] \{\phi_j\} = 0 \quad \text{and} \quad \{\phi_i\}^T [K] \{\phi_j\} = 0, \quad i \ne j$$

**Importance (1.5 Marks):**
1. It **decouples** the equations of motion, transforming the coupled N-DOF system into N independent single-DOF equations (modal superposition).
2. It allows the **diagonalization** of $[M]$ and $[K]$ via the modal transformation.
3. It dramatically reduces **computational cost** for large systems — essential for modern FE analysis of complex structures.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

### Question A

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Adapted]**
> **(a)** For a 2-DOF undamped spring-mass system with masses $m_1 = m_2 = m$ and stiffnesses $k_1 = k_2 = k_3 = k$ arranged in series (mass $m_1$ connected to wall by $k_1$, $m_1$ to $m_2$ by $k_2$, and $m_2$ to wall by $k_3$), derive the equation of motion and obtain the natural frequencies and mode shapes. **(7 Marks)**
>
> **(b)** Verify the orthogonality of the obtained mode shapes with respect to both the mass and stiffness matrices. Normalize the mode shapes with respect to the mass matrix. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution for Question A — Part (a)

**Step 1 — Formulate the EOM.** The equations of motion for the system are:

$$m \ddot{x}_1 + (k_1 + k_2) x_1 - k_2 x_2 = 0$$
$$m \ddot{x}_2 - k_2 x_1 + (k_2 + k_3) x_2 = 0$$

Substituting the given values $k_1 = k_2 = k_3 = k$:

$$m \ddot{x}_1 + 2k x_1 - k x_2 = 0$$
$$m \ddot{x}_2 - k x_1 + 2k x_2 = 0$$

In matrix form:

$$\begin{bmatrix} m & 0 \\ 0 & m \end{bmatrix} \begin{Bmatrix} \ddot{x}_1 \\ \ddot{x}_2 \end{Bmatrix} + \begin{bmatrix} 2k & -k \\ -k & 2k \end{bmatrix} \begin{Bmatrix} x_1 \\ x_2 \end{Bmatrix} = \begin{Bmatrix} 0 \\ 0 \end{Bmatrix}$$

**Step 2 — Assume Harmonic Solution.** $\{x\} = \{\phi\} \sin(\omega t)$ gives:

$$\begin{bmatrix} 2k - m\omega^2 & -k \\ -k & 2k - m\omega^2 \end{bmatrix} \begin{Bmatrix} \phi_1 \\ \phi_2 \end{Bmatrix} = \begin{Bmatrix} 0 \\ 0 \end{Bmatrix}$$

**Step 3 — Frequency Equation.** Setting the determinant to zero:

$$(2k - m\omega^2)^2 - k^2 = 0$$
$$(2k - m\omega^2)^2 = k^2$$
$$2k - m\omega^2 = \pm k$$

**Step 4 — Natural Frequencies.**
$$m\omega_1^2 = 2k - k = k \quad \Rightarrow \quad \boxed{\omega_1 = \sqrt{\dfrac{k}{m}}}$$
$$m\omega_2^2 = 2k + k = 3k \quad \Rightarrow \quad \boxed{\omega_2 = \sqrt{\dfrac{3k}{m}}}$$

**Step 5 — Mode Shapes.**
For $\omega_1^2 = k/m$: From the first row, $(2k - k)\phi_{1,1} - k\phi_{1,2} = 0 \Rightarrow \phi_{1,2} = \phi_{1,1}$.
$$\boxed{\{\phi_1\} = \begin{Bmatrix} 1 \\ 1 \end{Bmatrix}}$$

For $\omega_2^2 = 3k/m$: $(2k - 3k)\phi_{2,1} - k\phi_{2,2} = 0 \Rightarrow \phi_{2,2} = -\phi_{2,1}$.
$$\boxed{\{\phi_2\} = \begin{Bmatrix} 1 \\ -1 \end{Bmatrix}}$$

#### Model Solution for Question A — Part (b)

**Step 1 — Mass Orthogonality.**

$$\{\phi_1\}^T [M] \{\phi_2\} = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 1 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} m & 0 \\ 0 & m \end{bmatrix} \begin{Bmatrix} 1 \\ -1 \end{Bmatrix} = m(1) + m(-1) = 0 \quad \checkmark$$

**Step 2 — Stiffness Orthogonality.**

$$\{\phi_1\}^T [K] \{\phi_2\} = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 1 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} 2k & -k \\ -k & 2k \end{bmatrix} \begin{Bmatrix} 1 \\ -1 \end{Bmatrix}$$

$$= (2k - k)(1) + (-k + 2k)(-1) = k - k = 0 \quad \checkmark$$

**Step 3 — Mass-Normalize.** Compute the modal mass:

$$M_1 = \{\phi_1\}^T [M] \{\phi_1\} = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 1 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} m & 0 \\ 0 & m \end{bmatrix} \begin{Bmatrix} 1 \\ 1 \end{Bmatrix} = 2m$$

$$M_2 = \{\phi_2\}^T [M] \{\phi_2\} = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & -1 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} m & 0 \\ 0 & m \end{bmatrix} \begin{Bmatrix} 1 \\ -1 \end{Bmatrix} = 2m$$

The mass-normalized mode shapes are:

$$\{\psi_1\} = \frac{1}{\sqrt{2m}} \begin{Bmatrix} 1 \\ 1 \end{Bmatrix}, \qquad \{\psi_2\} = \frac{1}{\sqrt{2m}} \begin{Bmatrix} 1 \\ -1 \end{Bmatrix}$$

**Verification:** $\{\psi_1\}^T [M] \{\psi_1\} = \dfrac{1}{2m}(2m) = 1 \quad \checkmark$ and $\{\psi_1\}^T [M] \{\psi_2\} = 0 \quad \checkmark$

---

### Question B (Alternative Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Adapted]**
> **(a)** Starting from the equations of motion of an undamped 2-DOF system $[M]\{\ddot{x}\} + [K]\{x\} = \{0\}$, show rigorously that the mode shapes satisfy the orthogonality relations $\{\phi_i\}^T [M] \{\phi_j\} = 0$ and $\{\phi_i\}^T [K] \{\phi_j\} = 0$ for $i \ne j$. State clearly all assumptions made. **(7 Marks)**
>
> **(b)** With reference to the 2-DOF system of Question A, construct the modal matrix $[\Phi]$ and demonstrate that $\Phi^T M \Phi$ and $\Phi^T K \Phi$ are both diagonal matrices. Comment on the significance of this result. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution for Question B — Part (a)

**Assumptions (2 Marks):**
1. The system is **linear** (constant $[M]$ and $[K]$).
2. The system is **undamped** (no $[C]$ matrix).
3. The system is in **free vibration** (no forcing function).
4. The matrices $[M]$ and $[K]$ are **real, symmetric, and positive-definite**.
5. The natural frequencies are **distinct** ($\omega_i \ne \omega_j$ for $i \ne j$).

**Proof (5 Marks):** See Section 3.2 of these notes for the complete 9-step derivation. The key logical chain is:

- Start from $[K]\{\phi_i\} = \omega_i^2 [M]\{\phi_i\}$
- Pre-multiply by $\{\phi_j\}^T$ and also derive the analogous equation for $j$.
- Use symmetry to equate left-hand sides.
- Factor out $(\omega_i^2 - \omega_j^2) \ne 0$ to conclude $\{\phi_i\}^T [M]\{\phi_j\} = 0$.
- Substitute into the other equation to get $\{\phi_i\}^T [K]\{\phi_j\} = 0$.

#### Model Solution for Question B — Part (b)

**Step 1 — Construct Modal Matrix.**

$$[\Phi] = \begin{bmatrix} \{\phi_1\} & \{\phi_2\} \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 1 \\ 1 & -1 \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 2 — Compute $\Phi^T [M] \Phi$.**

$$\Phi^T [M] \Phi = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 1 \\ 1 & -1 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} m & 0 \\ 0 & m \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 1 \\ 1 & -1 \end{bmatrix}$$

$$= \begin{bmatrix} m & m \\ m & -m \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 1 \\ 1 & -1 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 2m & 0 \\ 0 & 2m \end{bmatrix} \quad \text{(Diagonal)} \quad \checkmark$$

**Step 3 — Compute $\Phi^T [K] \Phi$.**

$$\Phi^T [K] \Phi = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 1 \\ 1 & -1 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} 2k & -k \\ -k & 2k \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 1 \\ 1 & -1 \end{bmatrix}$$

$$= \begin{bmatrix} k & k \\ 3k & -3k \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 1 \\ 1 & -1 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 2k & 0 \\ 0 & 6k \end{bmatrix} \quad \text{(Diagonal)} \quad \checkmark$$

**Step 4 — Comment on Significance (2 Marks).**
- The off-diagonal terms vanish — the modal coordinates are **uncoupled**.
- $\Phi^T [M] \Phi = \text{diag}(M_1, M_2)$ and $\Phi^T [K] \Phi = \text{diag}(K_1, K_2) = \text{diag}(\omega_1^2 M_1, \omega_2^2 M_2)$.
- This permits the use of **modal superposition**, where the N-coupled equations of motion reduce to N independent SDOF equations. The time-history response of each mass is then obtained by combining the responses of the individual modes.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Sign Ambiguity:** Mode shapes are *unique only up to a multiplicative constant*. Many students lose marks by insisting on a specific sign; both $\{\phi_i\}$ and $-\{\phi_i\}$ are acceptable.
> 2. **Forgetting the Transpose:** A common error is writing $\{\phi_i\}[M]\{\phi_j\}$ instead of $\{\phi_i\}^T[M]\{\phi_j\}$. The former is a $1 \times 1$ "sandwich" only because the outer terms are column and row vectors respectively — but the **transpose must be shown** for full credit.
> 3. **Skipping the Distinctness Assumption:** The proof requires $\omega_i \ne \omega_j$. Always state this explicitly.
> 4. **Determinant Errors:** A single arithmetic slip in expanding the $2 \times 2$ determinant can cost 2–3 marks. Cross-check by verifying the trace-sum and product-sum identities: $\sum \omega_i^2 = \text{tr}(M^{-1}K)$ and $\prod \omega_i^2 = \det(M^{-1}K)$.
> 5. **Confusing Natural Frequency with Natural Circular Frequency:** $\omega_n$ is in rad/s; $f_n = \omega_n / (2\pi)$ is in Hz. KTU questions often specify the unit clearly — do not mix them up.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist (Must-Memorize for KTU Exams):**

- **The Generalized Eigenvalue Equation:** $\left( [K] - \omega^2 [M] \right) \{\phi\} = \{0\}$ is the heart of MDOF free-vibration analysis.
- **The Frequency Equation:** $\det([K] - \omega^2 [M]) = 0$ is a polynomial of degree $N$ whose $N$ positive real roots are $\omega_1^2, \omega_2^2, \ldots, \omega_N^2$.
- **Mode shapes are *relative*, not absolute.** Only the *ratio* of components is meaningful; absolute scaling is arbitrary.
- **Orthogonality holds with respect to BOTH** $[M]$ and $[K]$, *not* with respect to the identity matrix.
- **Distinct $\omega_i$** is a *prerequisite* for the standard orthogonality proof; repeated eigenvalues require special treatment.
- **Mass normalization** ($\{\psi_i\} = \{\phi_i\}/\sqrt{M_i}$) gives the unit-orthonormal basis, simplifying the modal stiffness to $\omega_i^2 \delta_{ij}$.
- **First mode** is always the *lowest* frequency and has *no internal nodes*; the $n$-th mode has $(n-1)$ internal nodes.
- **The total number of natural frequencies equals the number of DOFs.** No more, no less.
- **Reciprocity / Maxwell's theorem** tells us that the stiffness matrix is symmetric, which is what makes orthogonality derivable.
- **Practical solver tip:** For N ≥ 3, hand-calculation becomes impractical. KTU questions typically use $N = 2$ for analytical tractability.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Rayleigh's Method, Dunkarley's Method, Stodola's Method, Distributed Mass (Continuous) Systems
topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECET741 - STRUCTURAL DYNAMICS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Rayleigh's Method, Dunkarley's Method, Stodola's Method, Distributed Mass (Continuous) Systems

# MODULE 4: APPROXIMATE & EXACT METHODS FOR NATURAL FREQUENCIES

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Introduction to Approximate Methods

In structural dynamics, determining the **natural frequencies** and **mode shapes** of a multi-degree-of-freedom (MDOF) or continuous system is fundamental. Exact solution of the eigenvalue problem is often mathematically tedious, especially for large systems. To overcome this, engineers rely on **approximate methods** that trade minor loss of precision for huge gains in computational simplicity.

> [!NOTE]
> **Core Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus):**
> **Rayleigh's Method** is an energy-based approximate technique that yields the *fundamental* (lowest) natural frequency of a vibrating system by equating maximum **kinetic energy** to maximum **potential energy** (or using work-energy balance). It always produces an **upper bound** to the true fundamental frequency because the assumed shape is, in general, not the true mode shape.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Dunkerley's Method (Lower Bound Theorem):**
> Dunkerley's Method provides a **lower bound** to the fundamental natural frequency. It cleverly combines the individual contributions of each mass when treated as a *single-degree-of-freedom* system acting independently. The combined formula $\frac{1}{\omega_1^2} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \frac{1}{\omega_{i}^{2}}$ is famously called the **Dunkerley Equation**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Stodola's Method (Iterative Matrix Method):**
> Stodola's Method is a **successive iteration technique** to determine the *fundamental mode shape* and natural frequency of a discrete system. It repeatedly applies the **flexibility matrix** $[D]$ to a trial vector until it converges to the lowest mode. When applied repeatedly with deflation, *all* mode shapes and frequencies can be extracted.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Distributed Mass (Continuous) Systems:**
> A continuous system has its mass **infinitely distributed** along its length (e.g., a uniform beam, shaft, string, or rod). It has **infinite degrees of freedom**; hence it possesses an **infinite number of natural frequencies** and **continuous mode shapes** described by trigonometric / transcendental functions derived from a 4th-order Partial Differential Equation (PDE).

---

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> **Real-World Analogy — The "Guitar String" Story:**
> Imagine plucking a guitar string. The *fundamental* tone you hear corresponds to the **Rayleigh estimate** (the dominant, first mode). If you press your finger lightly along the string (adding artificial "stiffness constraints"), the pitch *rises* — this is Dunkerley's idea: each added constraint brings the frequency **down** to a *safe lower bound*, used for design. **Stodola's Method** is like repeatedly pulling the string into a new shape, releasing it, and watching it "settle" into its preferred vibration pattern — eventually, only the lowest mode survives the iteration. Finally, the *distributed mass* system is the realistic guitar string itself, where every atom of the wire is a tiny mass participating in the dance.

### 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

> [!NOTE]
> **Important Engineering Quantities:**
> - **Natural frequency** $\omega_n$: rad/s
> - **Cyclic frequency** $f_n$: Hz
> - **Conversion:** $f_n = \omega_n / 2\pi$
> - **Stiffness coefficient** $k$: N/m
> - **Mass per unit length** $\bar{m}$: kg/m (for distributed systems)
> - **Flexibility influence coefficient** $\delta_{ij}$: m/N (displacement at $i$ due to unit load at $j$)
> - **Standard acceleration due to gravity:** $g = 9.81 \, \text{m/s}^2$

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Rayleigh's Method — The Energy Balance Principle

**Theoretical Foundation:**
Rayleigh's Method is rooted in the **Principle of Conservation of Energy**. For an undamped, freely vibrating system, the total mechanical energy is constant. At the instant of **maximum displacement** (turning point), velocity = 0, so:

$$E_{total} = E_{potential,max} = \frac{1}{2} \int_0^L EI \left( \frac{\partial^2 y}{\partial x^2} \right)^2 dx$$

At the instant of **maximum velocity** (passing through equilibrium), displacement = 0, so:

$$E_{total} = E_{kinetic,max} = \frac{1}{2} \int_0^L \bar{m}(x) \dot{y}_{max}^{2} dx$$

**Why does Rayleigh's method always give an UPPER bound?**

When we assume a deflection shape $y(x)$ that is *not* the true mode shape, the kinetic energy of the assumed motion is generally **less** than the true kinetic energy, but the potential energy is over-estimated. Since $\omega^2 \propto E_{potential}/E_{kinetic}$, the ratio increases, giving $\omega_{Rayleigh} \geq \omega_{true}$.

**Key Steps (Logic Flow):**
1. **Assume** a plausible deflection shape $y(x)$ that satisfies the *geometric* (essential) boundary conditions.
2. Compute the **maximum strain energy** $U_{max} = \frac{1}{2}\int_0^L EI \left(\frac{d^2y}{dx^2}\right)^2 dx$.
3. Compute the **maximum kinetic energy** $T_{max} = \frac{1}{2}\omega^2 \int_0^L \bar{m}(x) y^2(x) dx$.
4. Equate $T_{max} = U_{max}$ and **solve for $\omega^2$**.
5. The closer the assumed shape to the true mode, the more accurate the answer.

**Rayleigh's Quotient (General Form):**

$$\boxed{\omega^2 = \dfrac{\displaystyle\int_0^L EI \left(\dfrac{d^2y}{dx^2}\right)^2 dx}{\displaystyle\int_0^L \bar{m}(x) y^2(x) dx}}$$

**Alternative (Discrete Systems):**

$$\boxed{\omega^2 = \dfrac{\phi^T [K] \phi}{\phi^T [M] \phi}}$$

where $\phi$ is the assumed mode shape vector, $[K]$ is the stiffness matrix, and $[M]$ is the mass matrix.

---

### 2.2 Dunkerley's Method — The Lower Bound Estimate

**Theoretical Foundation:**
Dunkerley considered a system with $n$ masses $m_1, m_2, \dots, m_n$ supported on a massless elastic structure of stiffness $k_{11}, k_{22}, \dots$ (each treated as a SDOF acting independently on the flexible support). By superposing the inertial effects:

**Key Logic Steps:**
1. Treat each mass $m_i$ as a SDOF oscillator, *ignoring* all other masses.
2. Calculate the SDOF natural frequency of each: $\omega_i^2 = \dfrac{k_i}{m_i}$.
3. Use the **Dunkerley superposition formula**:

$$\boxed{\dfrac{1}{\omega_1^2} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \dfrac{1}{\omega_{i}^2} = \dfrac{1}{\omega_{11}^2} + \dfrac{1}{\omega_{22}^2} + \cdots + \dfrac{1}{\omega_{nn}^2}}$$

4. The result $\omega_1$ is a **lower bound** to the true fundamental frequency.

**Modified Dunkerley Formula (using deflections):**

$$\dfrac{1}{\omega_1^2} \approx \sum_{i=1}^{n} m_i \, y_i$$

where $y_i$ is the static deflection at the location of mass $m_i$ due to the weight of all masses (or due to a unit load pattern). This is the **deflection form** of Dunkerley's equation.

**Why does it give a LOWER bound?**
Because assuming the masses act *independently* (decoupled) ignores the stiffening interaction between them, effectively treating the structure as *more flexible* than it actually is. A more flexible system vibrates at a *lower* frequency.

---

### 2.3 Stodola's Method — Iterative Eigenvalue Extraction

**Theoretical Foundation:**
Stodola's Method operates in the **modal (frequency) domain** using the *flexibility matrix* $[D]$ (inverse of stiffness, $[D] = [K]^{-1}$). The governing eigenvalue problem is:

$$[D][M]\{\phi\} = \dfrac{1}{\omega^2}\{\phi\}$$

or, in standard eigenvalue form:

$$\left([D][M] - \lambda I\right)\{\phi\} = 0, \quad \lambda = \dfrac{1}{\omega^2}$$

**Key Logic Steps:**
1. **Form** the flexibility matrix $[D]_{n \times n}$ where $D_{ij}$ = deflection at $i$ due to unit load at $j$.
2. **Form** the mass matrix $[M]_{n \times n}$ (usually diagonal).
3. Compute the **dynamic matrix** $[A] = [D][M]$.
4. **Assume** a trial vector $\{\phi^{(0)}\}$ (e.g., all ones).
5. **Iterate**: $\{Y^{(k+1)}\} = [A]\{\phi^{(k)}\}$ then normalize.
6. **Converge**: The ratio of corresponding components in successive iterations gives the **dominant eigenvalue** $\lambda_1 = 1/\omega_1^2$.
7. **Deflate** to find higher modes: $[A]_1 = [A] - \lambda_1 \frac{\{\phi_1\}\{\phi_1\}^T[M]}{\{\phi_1\}^T[M]\{\phi_1\}}$.

**Convergence Rule:** Converges to the *largest* eigenvalue of $[A]$, which corresponds to the *smallest* natural frequency $\omega_1$ (since $\lambda = 1/\omega^2$).

**Convergence Speed:** Geometric, with rate $\lambda_2/\lambda_1 = (\omega_1/\omega_2)^2$. Slower if modes are closely spaced.

---

### 2.4 Distributed Mass (Continuous) Systems

**Governing PDE for Flexural Vibration of a Uniform Beam:**

$$EI \dfrac{\partial^4 y(x,t)}{\partial x^4} + \bar{m} \dfrac{\partial^2 y(x,t)}{\partial t^2} = 0$$

**Separation of Variables** $y(x,t) = \phi(x) \cdot q(t)$ gives:

$$q(t) = C_1 \cos \omega t + C_2 \sin \omega t$$
$$\dfrac{d^4 \phi}{dx^4} - \beta^4 \phi = 0, \quad \text{where} \quad \beta^4 = \dfrac{\omega^2 \bar{m}}{EI}$$

**General Solution:**

$$\phi(x) = A\cos\beta x + B\sin\beta x + C\cosh\beta x + D\sinh\beta x$$

Boundary conditions on $\phi$ and $\phi''$ (or shear/moment) yield the **frequency equation** $\Delta(\beta L) = 0$.

**Standard Cases:**

| Boundary Conditions | Frequency Equation | Fundamental $\beta_1 L$ | Mode Shape $\phi_1(x)$ |
|---|---|---|---|
| Simply Supported | $\sin\beta L = 0$ | $\pi$ | $\sin(\pi x/L)$ |
| Cantilever (Fixed-Free) | $\cos\beta L \cosh\beta L = -1$ | $1.875$ | $\cosh\beta x - \cos\beta x - \sigma(\sinh\beta x - \sin\beta x)$ |
| Fixed-Fixed | $\cos\beta L \cosh\beta L = 1$ | $4.730$ | $\cosh\beta x - \cos\beta x - \sigma(\sinh\beta x - \sin\beta x)$ |
| Free-Free | $\cos\beta L \cosh\beta L = 1$ | $4.730$ | symmetric/antisymmetric |

where $\sigma = \dfrac{\cosh\beta L - \cos\beta L}{\sinh\beta L - \sin\beta L}$

**Natural Frequency Formula:**

$$\boxed{\omega_n = \left(\beta_n L\right)^2 \sqrt{\dfrac{EI}{\bar{m} L^4}}}$$

---

### 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet (High-Yield Cheat Sheet)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **TABLE A — Approximate Method Formulas**

| Method | Formula | Type of Bound | Best Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| Rayleigh | $\omega^2 = \frac{\int_0^L EI (y'')^2 dx}{\int_0^L \bar{m} y^2 dx}$ | Upper Bound | Fundamental $\omega_1$ with assumed shape |
| Rayleigh (Discrete) | $\omega^2 = \frac{\phi^T K \phi}{\phi^T M \phi}$ | Upper Bound | Quick estimate for MDOF |
| Dunkerley | $\frac{1}{\omega_1^2} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \frac{1}{\omega_{ii}^2}$ | Lower Bound | Design safety (worst case flexibility) |
| Dunkerley (Deflection) | $\frac{1}{\omega_1^2} \approx \sum_{i=1}^{n} m_i \delta_i$ | Lower Bound | Quick estimate with static deflection |
| Stodola | $\{\phi_{k+1}\} = [D][M]\{\phi_k\}$ | Converges to Exact | MDOF fundamental mode |
| Stodola Eigenvalue | $\lambda_1 = \frac{1}{\omega_1^2} = \frac{\phi_i^{(k+1)}}{\phi_i^{(k)}}$ | Exact as $k \to \infty$ | All modes (with deflation) |

> **TABLE B — Continuous Beam Frequency Equation**

| System | $\beta_n L$ values | Natural Frequency |
|---|---|---|
| SS-SS | $n\pi$ | $\omega_n = \frac{n^2 \pi^2}{L^2}\sqrt{\frac{EI}{\bar{m}}}$ |
| Cantilever | $1.875,\ 4.694,\ 7.855,\ 10.996$ | $\omega_n = (\beta_n L)^2 \sqrt{\frac{EI}{\bar{m} L^4}}$ |
| Fixed-Fixed | $4.730,\ 7.853,\ 10.996$ | Same form |

---

### 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

> [!NOTE]
> - **Rayleigh's Method** is used in the *preliminary design* of tall buildings, chimneys, and bridges to estimate the fundamental period for seismic analysis.
> - **Dunkerley's Method** is invaluable in *rotating machinery* design (turbine shafts) to ensure that operating speeds stay *above* the lower bound estimate, providing a safety margin.
> - **Stodola's Method** remains a fundamental teaching tool in *hand-computation* of natural frequencies for small MDOF systems (e.g., 2- or 3-story shear buildings) and forms the conceptual basis of modern power iteration algorithms in FEA software.
> - **Continuous system analysis** is essential for slender members like *bridge girders, helicopter blades, turbine blades, and aircraft wings*, where lumping the mass introduces unacceptable error.
